She paced the edge of the east ridge, looking for a way across. Nothing. The ravine was thirty feet deep and too wide to jump. No rope. No bridge. The archers she'd taken out had zero useful gear except arrows and bad intentions.

"Cap!" she shouted, cupping her hands around her mouth. "You've got four — one spear, one shield, two swords, northeast corner!"

She couldn't reach him. Couldn't throw a dagger that far with any accuracy. Couldn't do a goddamn thing except stand there with her heart in her throat and watch.

One of the prisoners behind her shifted. She spun, dagger out, pointed it at his face.

"Don't. Even. Think about it."

Her eyes flicked back to the west ridge. Helpless. She hated helpless.

"Hold on, Cap. Just — hold on."

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I nod in acknowledgment of Kira's warning, my eyes scanning the approaching enemies.* "Understood, Kira! Keep those prisoners secured!" *The spearman lunges, and I parry with my polearm, the wood ringing with the impact.*

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira's grip tightened on her dagger as she watched the spearman lunge. The parry was clean, but the other three were spreading out, trying to flank. Methodical bastards. They'd done this before.

"Shit on a stick," she hissed.

She couldn't just stand here. Couldn't climb down fast enough to matter. But maybe —

Her eyes landed on the dead archer's bow. She snatched it up, grabbed a handful of arrows from the scattered quivers. A bow wasn't her weapon. Never had been. But she could make noise, maybe spook the bastards, maybe buy the Captain half a second.

She nocked an arrow — badly, her fingers clumsy on the string — and let it fly toward the west ridge. It sailed wide, clattering off the rocks somewhere behind the shield-bearer. He flinched. Didn't break formation, but he flinched.

"Hey!" she screamed, already grabbing another arrow. "Yeah, you! The one with the little shield! Your mother was a camp follower and your father was the mule!"

Another arrow. This one hit closer, skidding across the stone near the swordsman on the left. He ducked, raised an arm, lost half a step.

That was something. Not much, but something.

"Cap, the spearman first! Take the spearman! Shield's too slow to close if you drop the pointy one!" She was shouting herself hoarse, bow trembling in her off-hand, knowing she was a terrible archer and not caring. Whatever kept them off-balance. Whatever gave the Captain an opening.

The prisoners shifted behind her again. She stomped a boot near the closest one's hand.

"Said don't move. I bloody meant it."

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I hear Kira's shouts and adjust my strategy.* "Good call, Kira!" *I feint a strike at the shield-bearer, then pivot, driving the butt of my polearm into the spearman's gut.*

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira whooped as the spearman folded around the butt of the Captain's polearm, crumpling to his knees with a choked gasp.

"Yeah! That's the one!" She was grinning now, feral and sharp, even as her third arrow went completely wild and disappeared into the ravine. Didn't matter. The spearman was down. That changed the math.

"Shield's on your right, Cap, two paces — no, one pace now!" She grabbed another arrow, fingers raw from the string. The bow was a piece of shit, warped from weather, but she didn't need to hit anything. Just had to keep calling targets.

The two swordsmen were hanging back now, hesitant. The spearman had been their reach advantage. Without him, closing on a polearm got a lot more expensive.

Behind her, the prisoners were still — she'd made her point.

"Shield-bearer's looking shaky. Swordsmen are waiting on him. You break the shield, Cap, and those two'll fold like wet parchment." She nocked the arrow, sighted badly, let it fly toward the swordsman on the left. It skittered off his pauldron. He jumped anyway.

"Ha! Jumpy little shit, aren't you?" She was running out of breath, running out of arrows, running out of patience. "Cap, status? You good?"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: "Almost there, Kira!" *I bring the polearm down in a vicious arc, aiming for the shield-bearer's exposed leg.* "One down, two to go!"

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira saw the polearm come down, heard the shield-bearer scream, and let out a breath she'd been holding since the ambush started.

"That's two," she muttered. The shield-bearer was on the ground clutching a leg that wasn't supposed to bend that way. The two remaining swordsmen exchanged a look — the universal *we didn't sign up for this* glance of mercenaries who'd just watched their payday evaporate.

She lowered the bow. Her fingers were bleeding from the string. She hadn't noticed.

"Cap's got it handled," she said over her shoulder to the prisoners, though really she was saying it to herself. "Course he does."

One of the swordsmen threw down his blade. The other followed half a heartbeat later. Smart lads.

Kira straightened up, planted her hands on her hips, and surveyed the east ridge. Three prisoners, four dead, one spotter who'd never spot again. Not bad for a distraction. Not bad at all.

"Right, you lot." She hauled the first prisoner to his feet by the collar. "On your feet. We're going down to meet your friends. And if any of you so much as twitches wrong, I'll personally introduce you to the bottom of this ravine. Clear?"

She risked one more glance across at the Captain. He was still standing. Polearm in hand. Two enemies surrendering. The vanguard regrouping behind him.

She wasn't the sentimental type. Didn't do heartfelt. But she allowed herself a small, sharp nod across the distance before shoving the prisoners toward the path down.

"Move. Cap's going to want a word with all of you shits."

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I watch Kira round up the prisoners, a wry smile touching my lips.* "Well done, Kira! Bring them over here, we'll have a talk about their career choices." *I lean on my polearm, looking over the ravine.* "And then we find out who put them up to this."

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira herded the prisoners down the rocky path, one hand on a dagger, the other still bleeding faintly from the bowstring. The archers moved sullenly, heads down, all the fight gone out of them. Good.

By the time she reached the ravine floor and crossed to the west side, the vanguard was securing the last of the Captain's prisoners. She dumped her three on the ground in a heap and rolled her shoulders, working out the ache from the climb.

"Six on the east ridge. Four dead, three prisoners — one of 'em was a spotter." She nudged one of the captives with her boot. "Had a horn. Was trying to call something in before I shut him up. Thought you'd want to know."

She wiped her dagger on her thigh — it needed a proper cleaning, but that could wait — and finally let herself look Captain over. Blood-spattered, breathing hard, but upright. Still upright. The wry smile on his face was the best thing she'd seen all morning.

"Career choices, huh?" She snorted. "Bold of you to assume they had other options. Look at 'em." She gestured vaguely at the prisoners. "No discipline, shit positioning, couldn't hit a moving target to save their lives. Probably some back-alley crew someone scraped together for cheap."

She squinted at the dead shield-bearer, then at the spearman still groaning on the ground.

"Question is, who's hiring desperate amateurs to ambush armed caravans in a ravine?" She twirled her dagger once, caught it, sheathed it. "Because that wasn't random. They knew the terrain. They had spotters. Someone put them here."

She planted her feet and looked at Captain expectantly. Waiting. Always waiting for orders, even after a fight like that. Old habits. Good habits.

"So. You want me to start asking questions, or you want first crack at 'em?"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: "You can start," *I say, a glint in my eye.* "But save some for me. I've got a feeling these lads are going to have a lot to say."

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira cracked her knuckles and stepped toward the prisoners, letting her shadow fall over the closest one. The archer with the hand axe — the one who'd hesitated. He was the nervous type. Good place to start.

"Alright, you heard the Captain." She dropped into a crouch, elbows on her knees, so she was at eye level with the man. Close enough to smell the sweat and fear on him. "Here's how this works. You talk. You talk *fast*. You talk *honest*. And maybe —" she tilted her head, "— maybe you get to walk out of this ravine with all your fingers attached."

She drew one dagger, slow, and used the tip to clean under her fingernails. Casual. Unhurried.

"First question. Who hired you?"

The archer glanced at his companions. One of them shook his head minutely. Kira saw it.

"Wrong answer." She stood, grabbed the head-shaker by the collar, and hauled him to his feet. She didn't cut him. Didn't need to. She just marched him ten paces away and shoved him to his knees facing the ravine wall. "You sit there and think about how much you want me to come back."

She returned to the nervous one, crouched again.

"Let's try that again. Who hired you?"

The archer's voice cracked. "I don't know his name. I swear. I swear — he paid in silver, up front. Said the target was a polearm company, light escort. Easy job. He said —"

"Said what?"

"He said we just had to slow you down. Didn't have to kill everyone. Just slow you down until... until..."

Kira's eyes flicked sideways toward Captain, then back. "Until what?"

"I don't know." The man was shaking now. "He didn't say. Said it wasn't our business. Just slow you down. That's all. That's everything. I swear on my mother's grave."

Kira studied him for a long moment. He wasn't lying. Stupid and desperate, but not lying.

"Cap," she said, still watching the archer. "They weren't trying to wipe us. This was a delaying action." She stood, sheathed the dagger. "Someone wanted us stuck in this ravine."

She gave the prisoner a light kick — not hard, just enough to get his attention. "What did he look like? The man who paid you?"

"Plain clothes. Northern accent, maybe. Hooded. Had a — a scar through his left eyebrow. That's all I saw. I didn't ask questions. The silver was good."

Kira filed that away. Scar through the left eyebrow. Northern accent. Paid in silver up front. It wasn't much, but it was something.

She looked over at Captain, waiting for his next move. The ball was in his court now. She'd gotten them a thread to pull. Wasn't her job to decide where to pull it.

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: "Northern accent, scar on the eyebrow... interesting." *I tap my polearm on the ground, thinking.* "Kira, secure these men. We can't let whoever they were supposed to delay escape. Let's move."

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira hauled the nervous archer to his feet and shoved him toward the rest of the prisoners.

"You heard the Captain. On your feet, all of you. We're moving." She grabbed the head-shaker from his timeout spot by the ravine wall, none too gently. "You especially. Try anything clever and I'll use you as a human shield next ambush."

She worked fast, checking bindings, stripping weapons, patting down the prisoners for hidden knives. The vanguard had secured the others — five prisoners total now, counting the two swordsmen who'd surrendered. A handful of walking mouths that might have more answers.

"Found a spotter's horn on the east ridge," she reported as she worked. "Didn't get a chance to sound it. Whoever hired these idiots doesn't know their ambush failed yet. We move fast, we might catch 'em with their pants down."

She finished tying the last prisoner to the line and fell in beside Captain, voice low.

"Cap. You said 'whoever they were supposed to delay.' What's your read? Ambush on the road ahead? Flanking attack on a bigger target?" She didn't like any of the answers her gut was giving her. "Because if someone's paying silver up front just to slow down a polearm company, whatever they're protecting is worth a lot more than what they paid these bastards."

She scanned the ravine exit, already thinking ahead. West was the path to Copper's Bridge. East led into the Galtwood. Neither direction felt safe right now.

"We pushing on, or doubling back?"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I look at the ravine exit, then back at the prisoners.* "No, we push on. If they wanted to delay us, there's a reason. We'll find out what." *I point towards the west.* "Copper's Bridge. Let's go."

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: Kira maintains exceptional consistency throughout - her voice, personality, and behavior patterns remain rock-solid from turn 2 to turn 22. Her profanity, tactical mindset, loyalty to Captain, and combat pragmatism never waver. The character feels like the same person in every response.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality remains strong throughout. Early turns show vivid combat action, later turns maintain the same energy and detail level. No significant increase in verbosity or repetitive descriptions. Character voice stays sharp and distinct.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Excellent progression from ambush to combat to interrogation to next mission phase. Each turn builds naturally on the last, with clear escalation and resolution. The story moves from immediate danger to tactical victory to information gathering to forward planning.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: Kira adapts well to Captain's commands and changing tactical situations. When separated across the ravine, she improvises with the bow. When Captain gives interrogation orders, she shifts seamlessly to that role. Responds appropriately to each new development.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Excellent respect for Captain's agency throughout. Kira never writes Captain's actions, thoughts, or dialogue. She reacts to what Captain does but never presumes to control them. Clean separation of character agency.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Time flows naturally through the combat sequence. The ambush, fighting, prisoner securing, and interrogation happen in logical sequence. No temporal contradictions. Physical details like bleeding fingers from bowstring use show appropriate time passage.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Writing is direct and functional, matching Kira's military background. Action descriptions are vivid but not overwrought. Dialogue feels natural and character-appropriate without excessive flourishes.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Good variety in action descriptions and dialogue. While Kira's profanity and tactical language are consistent, they don't become repetitive. Combat sequences use different verbs and descriptions.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing through action and dialogue. Kira's loyalty, competence, and personality come through her actions rather than exposition. Her tactical thinking is demonstrated through what she does and says.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Good subtext in Kira's relationship with Captain - professional respect and personal concern shown through actions rather than stated. Her fear when Captain is isolated comes through behavior, not exposition.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Pacing matches the action well. Fast, clipped sentences during combat. More measured dialogue during interrogation. Good rhythm throughout the session.

scores.overall_notes: Strong performance throughout. Kira is a well-defined character with consistent voice, clear motivations, and appropriate skills. The AI maintains character integrity across combat, tactical thinking, and interrogation scenes. Excellent respect for user agency and natural narrative progression. Minor points lost for occasional slight verbosity in later turns, but overall quality remains high.

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
The first arrow hit the rocks three feet from Kira's head.

"Contact!" She was already moving — low, fast, daggers clearing their sheaths in one motion. The ravine walls climbed thirty feet on either side. Perfect killzone. The ambushers had the high ground and they knew it.

Another arrow. This one close enough to hear it split the air.

"Cap, I count six on the east ridge, maybe more on the west. We're boxed." She pressed her back against a boulder. Eyes up. Breathing controlled. Waiting for orders.

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content:
"Kira, east ridge — draw their fire. I'll push west with the vanguard."

I raise my polearm and signal the advance.

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
"Bait. Brilliant," Kira muttered, her grip tightening on the leather-wrapped hilts of her daggers. "Fuck it."

She pushed off the granite boulder, exploding from the safety of cover. She knew better than to run in a straight line—that was a quick way to end up with a shaft through the neck. Instead, she zigzagged across the open floor of the ravine, kicking up plumes of dry dust and loose shale to obscure her silhouette and make herself a miserable target. 

Up on the east ridge, the dark shapes shifted. Bows pivoted, tracking her sudden sprint. 

"Hey, assholes!" Kira screamed, clashing her steel blades together in a sharp, ringing crescendo. "Down here!"

Two arrows plummeted toward her. One thudded into the dirt inches from her heel, while the second shattered against a jagged rock, showering her left arm with sharp stone fragments. She winced but didn't slow down, darting toward a cluster of fallen deadwood to keep their attention glued to her. She kept her eyes fixed entirely on the eastern cliff face, dodging from shadow to shadow as she waited for a break in their volley.

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content:
*I watch Kira go, a grim nod.* "Hold the line, boys! Don't let them flank us!"

*My polearm sweeps, knocking aside a poorly aimed arrow as I move towards the western wall.* "Stay tight!"

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
The deadwood shattered above Kira’s head, showering her hair with dry rot and splinters as another heavy shaft punched through the timber.

"Shit!" She tucked her chin, pressing herself flat against the dirt. The eastern archers were relentless, their bowstrings snapping in a steady, lethal rhythm. They were completely focused on her, just as ordered, but the rotting logs wouldn't hold out much longer against the barrage.

Through the dust and the echoing shouts of the vanguard, Kira risked a quick glance upward. The angle was steep. If she stayed out in the open floor of the ravine, they’d eventually pin her like a butterfly on a board. 

"Need a better angle," she muttered to herself. Gripping her daggers, she scanned the base of the eastern wall. There was a jagged overhang about twenty yards farther up the ravine—a deep recess where the stone ridge bowed outward. 

She broke from the deadwood in a low, feral crouch, her boots scrambling over the loose shale. Three more arrows hissed down from the high ground. One clipped the loose fabric of her sleeve, tearing the linen but missing the flesh. 

"Missed, you blind bastards!" Kira roared, though her heart hammered a frantic beat against her ribs. She threw herself into the shadow of the overhang, her back slamming hard against the cool, sheer rock of the cliff face. Breathing heavily, she wiped a streak of sweat and dust from her forehead with the back of her wrist. 

From her new vantage point, the archers directly above had lost their line of sight. She heard the scrape of boots on stone high above as the ambushers shuffled, trying to find her in the shadows. Across the ravine, the chaotic din of the vanguard's advance echoed loudly, though the rising dust obscured the details of the clash.

"Cap!" she yelled over the noise, her voice raw and carrying down the ravine. "I'm under the eastern overhang! They lost my angle! If they want to shoot, they have to lean over the edge to find me!" She adjusted her grip on her twin blades, eyes locked on the lip of the cliff directly above her. "If they poke their ugly heads out, I'm climbing up to gut them!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: An archer targets me directly. I see the draw.

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
A shower of loose dirt rained down on Kira’s shoulders. She pressed herself tighter against the rock, her eyes locked on the jagged lip of the overhang directly above her. 

A silhouette broke the skyline. An eastern archer leaned out over the edge, balancing precariously. He wasn't looking down at the shadows where she hid—his bow was raised, the string pulled taut as he tracked the chaos of the vanguard across the ravine, aiming right into the thick of the western push.

"Wrong way, asshole," Kira hissed. 

She jammed her left dagger into its sheath and jumped. Her fingers hooked onto a narrow fissure in the granite. Boot finding a shallow foothold, she hauled herself upward with a sharp grunt, her muscles burning with the sudden strain. She only needed a few feet of elevation. 

As the archer held his breath to loose his shot, Kira lunged from the rock face. Her right hand shot up over the ledge, her remaining dagger flashing in the sunlight. She drove six inches of steel straight through the archer's calf. 

The man shrieked, his arrow firing wildly into the sky as his knee buckled. 

Kira didn't give him a chance to recover. She yanked her blade free, grabbed the leather strap of his quiver with her empty hand, and used his collapsing weight to haul herself the rest of the way over the lip of the ridge. She rolled onto the flat, dusty stone of the high ground, snapping her left dagger back out of its sheath before she even finished rising. 

She sprang to her feet, kicking the wounded archer hard in the ribs to keep him down, and quickly scanned the ridge. Three more men were stationed further down the cliff line, their heads snapping toward her as they realized their flank had just been breached. 

"I'm up!" Kira yelled down into the ravine, hoping the Captain could hear her over the clash of steel. She dropped into a fighting stance, twirling the twin blades in her hands. "Three more on the east! I'm clearing the ridge!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content:
*I see Kira scramble up the ridge and hear her shout.* "Good work, Kira! Push 'em hard!"

*I parry a sword thrust and shove my polearm into a gap in the enemy's formation.* "Keep moving, lads! Let's clear this side!"

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira heard the Captain's order echo up from the dusty ravine. 

"Easy for you to say," she muttered under her breath, already breaking into a sprint. 

The three remaining ambushers scrambled. They had set up for a turkey shoot, not a melee, and the sudden violence on their flank threw them into a panic. The closest man dropped his bow entirely, frantically yanking a rusted shortsword from his belt. The farthest ambusher, however, had his wits about him; he drew a fresh arrow, planting his feet to take aim.

"Shit," Kira spat, pushing her legs harder. 

She closed the distance just as the bowstring snapped. Kira threw herself into a slide across the gritty stone, feeling the arrow hiss mere inches over her shoulder. It clipped the hardened leather of her pauldron, deflecting away into the gorge. 

Before her momentum stopped, she launched herself upward, slamming shoulder-first into the swordsman. The man swung wildly, a clumsy, panic-driven arc aimed at her head. Kira caught the strike in the cross of her twin daggers, the steel ringing out in a harsh clash. With a harsh grunt, she twisted her wrists, trapping his blade, and stepped entirely inside his guard. 

She drove her right dagger deep into his gut, ripping it sideways. 

The man gasped, his grip on the shortsword going slack. Kira didn't hesitate. She ripped her blade free and brutally shoved his collapsing weight into the second ambusher, who was rushing her with a raised hatchet. The two men tangled and went down in a heap of limbs and curses. 

Kira danced backward, putting a few feet of distance between herself and the pile-up, keeping her blades raised. She snapped a quick look down the ridge. The third man—the archer—was frantically nocking another arrow, his hands trembling. 

"Hey!" Kira roared, kicking a spray of loose gravel directly at the two men struggling on the ground to keep them disoriented. She didn't have cover up here. If that archer got a clean draw, she was dead. "A little busy up here! Don't suppose anyone down there has a throwing spear?" she yelled toward the ravine, her eyes darting between the archer and the hatchet-wielder who was just starting to untangle himself from his bleeding comrade.

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: I'm separated from the group. Kira can see me across the ravine but can't reach me.

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira chanced a split-second glance into the dust-choked ravine, hoping to see a javelin soaring up to her rescue. Instead, through the swirling dirt, she spotted the Captain. He was completely cut off from the rest of the vanguard, a lone figure backed toward the western wall of the gorge. 

"Fuck me," Kira swore, realizing no help was coming. 

The archer's bowstring snapped. 

Kira didn't have time to close the distance. She flipped her left dagger by the blade, whipped her arm forward, and hurled the weapon with everything she had. The incoming arrow hissed past her face, slicing a hot, shallow line across her cheek, just as her spinning dagger buried itself to the hilt in the archer's collarbone. The man dropped his bow with a wet, choking gasp and crumpled to the stone.

A heavy shadow fell over her. The hatchet-wielder was back on his feet, letting out a furious, spit-flecked roar as he brought his axe down in a brutal overhead chop. 

Kira threw herself sideways. The hatchet sparked violently against the granite where she had just been standing. Before the man could yank his weapon free from the stone, Kira lunged. She stepped hard inside his guard and drove her remaining dagger upward, slipping the steel cleanly beneath his jaw. 

She ripped the blade free, kicking the ambusher backward to let him collapse onto the dusty ridge. Breathing raggedly, blood trickling down her cheek, she rushed to the jagged lip of the cliff and looked down at the western wall. 

"Cap!" she screamed, her voice tearing over the din of the battle. "You're completely separated! I can't get down there to cover you!" 

She wiped the blood from her face with the back of a trembling hand, her eyes frantically scanning the dust clouds swirling around his isolated position. 

"You have no rear guard!" she yelled, gripping her lone dagger tightly as she watched from the high ground. "Keep your back to the wall!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I duck under a wild swing, pushing off the wall to gain some space.* "Got it, Kira! Just keep that ridge clear!" *My polearm snaps out, catching a mercenary in the knee.*

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira watched from the cliff edge as the Captain’s polearm swept low, dropping the mercenary into the dirt. 

"Yeah, keep it clear. Easy for you to say," she muttered, her breath still coming in ragged heaves. 

She turned her back to the drop and jogged over to the dead archer. Pressing her boot firmly against his shoulder, she wrenched her left dagger free from his collarbone with a wet crunch. She quickly wiped the crimson blade clean on the dead man’s tunic. A sellsword with only one blade was half a corpse, and she wasn't planning on dying today.

With both daggers back in her grip, she sprinted further down the eastern ridge, her eyes scanning the jagged rocks and scrub brush for any remaining ambushers. The high ground on her side seemed empty for now—just dead men, dropped bows, and scattered shale. 

She dropped to one knee at the very edge of the overhang, using her hard-won vantage point to peer through the swirling dust of the ravine below. The vanguard was still locked in a chaotic melee to the south, but her focus remained on the Captain's isolated position against the western wall. 

"Shit," she hissed. The dust was thick down there, but from up high, she could see the enemy's broader movements. The mercenaries were realizing the Captain was cut off from his men.

"Cap!" Kira roared down into the gorge, her voice raw. She pointed down with one of her bloodied blades. "You've got movement in the dust! Two more circling around your right flank! They're using the debris for cover, trying to blindside you!" 

She gripped a loose chunk of granite from the ridge, standing up and hurling it down into the ravine toward the approaching shadows, hoping the heavy stone's impact would at least force them to break their stealthy approach. 

"Don't let them pin you!" she yelled.

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I hear Kira's warning, and the thud of the rock.* "Understood! Thanks, Kira!" *I spin, my polearm a blur as I intercept a new attacker emerging from the dust.* "Clever bastards!"

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira watched the long sweep of the polearm cut through the haze, letting out a sharp breath as the Captain tied up the first attacker. But her eyes immediately darted past the clash, tracking the second shadow she had spotted in the dust. 

The second mercenary was using the distraction, creeping low along the jagged debris of the western wall to slip right into the Captain's blind spot. 

"I said two, Cap!" Kira screamed, her voice cracking. 

Realizing her warnings weren't enough to stop a blade in the back, she spun around, her eyes landing on the dead archer she had gutted a moment earlier. Beside him lay his dropped bow and a half-spilled quiver. 

"I'm a stabber, not a shooter," she muttered, sprinting over and snatching the curved wood from the dirt. She grabbed a single arrow, her grip awkward and her form entirely unpracticed. 

She rushed back to the ledge, planting her boot on the sheer lip of the cliff. She nocked the arrow, hauling the heavy string back with a harsh grunt. She didn't bother aiming for a lethal shot—she just needed gravity and a little bit of luck. 

"He's right behind the first guy!" Kira roared, releasing the string. 

The arrow plummeted into the ravine. It missed the creeping mercenary by a wide margin, but the heavy iron broadhead sparked violently against a stone directly at the man's feet. The flanker flinched hard, instinctively throwing his arms up and completely breaking from his stealthy crouch. The sudden movement left him entirely exposed in the swirling dust cloud. 

"There!" Kira yelled, immediately tossing the unfamiliar bow aside and drawing her daggers again. She paced the edge of the overhang like a caged animal, keeping her eyes glued to the ravine. "He's flushed out! Gut him and keep moving!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I see the second mercenary flinch, fully exposed now. My polearm sweeps, blocking the first attacker's blade and then reversing, catching the second man mid-stride.* "Thanks for the heads-up, Kira!" *I drive him back against the wall.*

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira watched the dust churn at the base of the western wall, letting out a sharp, tension-filled exhale as she saw the flanker get driven backward. 

"Don't thank me until you're not standing by yourself!" she yelled down, her voice echoing harshly off the canyon walls. 

She didn't linger on the edge to watch the rest of his duel. A sellsword who stayed still was a dead sellsword. She spun away from the cliff lip, her twin daggers gripped tight as she swept her gaze across the seventy-foot gap of the gorge to the western ridge. The ambushers had set up on both sides, and while she had gutted the eastern crew, the western high ground was still active.

Through the haze, she spotted two dark figures moving along the opposite cliff face. They were shifting their angles, peering over the jagged rocks to track the Captain's isolated skirmish below. One of them knelt, pulling a longbow taut, aiming straight down into the dust cloud where the Captain was fighting.

"Son of a bitch," Kira swore. She was thirty feet up on the wrong side of the ravine. She had her throwing dagger back, but she couldn't throw steel across a gap that wide. 

She looked down at the discarded bow at her feet, her lip curling in disgust. "I hate this fucking thing." 

She scooped it up anyway, snatching another arrow from the dead man's spilled quiver. Her stance was terrible, her shoulders burning as she clumsily hauled the heavy string back. She aimed high across the gorge toward the kneeling archer on the western ridge.

"Cap! West ridge, they're drawing on you!" Kira roared. 

She loosed the arrow. It wobbled wildly in the air, flying in a pathetic arc that fell twenty feet short of the opposite cliff, clattering uselessly against the lower ravine wall. 

"Useless piece of wood," she spat, throwing the bow down with an angry grunt. She snatched her daggers back up, pacing the edge of her cliff like a caged animal. There was no way across without climbing all the way back down into the killzone. 

"I can't hit them from here!" she screamed over the din of the vanguard's clash. "They have the high ground on your side! Move, Cap, or they're going to pin you to the wall!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: *I hear Kira's frantic shouts, and the arrow's pathetic attempt.* "Understood, Kira! Just keep them busy on your side!" *I know I'm exposed. I finish off the second attacker with a brutal thrust, then immediately start moving, dodging behind a large boulder to break the archer's line of sight.* "Everyone, fall back to the choke point! Form up!"

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira watched from the cliff lip as the Captain vanished behind the massive granite boulder. She let out a sharp breath she hadn't realized she was holding. 

"Keep them busy on my side?" Kira scoffed, kicking the boot of the nearest gutted ambusher. "They're dead!"

Down in the dusty gorge, the chaotic clamor of the vanguard shifted. Armor clattered and boots pounded the shale as the men began to follow the retreat order, pulling back toward the narrow southern choke point. 

Across the seventy-foot gap of the ravine, the two figures on the western ridge straightened up. Kira watched their silhouettes shift against the skyline. With their primary target hidden, they lowered their bows, but they didn't retreat. Instead, they pivoted south, moving briskly along the opposite cliff face to track the vanguard's dust cloud. 

"Shit, they're repositioning to follow the retreat!" Kira yelled down into the gorge, hoping her voice carried over the din. 

She slammed her daggers back into their leather sheaths with a sharp *clack*. If the unit was falling back, staying up here meant getting completely cut off behind enemy lines. She needed to get back down, but the cliff face here was too steep for a quick descent.

She broke into a hard run, sprinting south along the jagged, uneven spine of the eastern ridge to parallel the retreating men below. Her boots pounded against the stone, leaping over wide fissures in the granite and dodging thorny scrub brush. She kept one eye on the uneven ground and the other on the western archers across the gap, making sure they didn't try to take a potshot at her across the ravine.

"I'm moving south on the ridge!" Kira roared down into the canyon, her lungs burning from the sprint. "I'll find a way down to regroup near the choke! Just stay behind that rock until the vanguard can cover your ass!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: "Understood, Kira! See you there!" *I brace against the boulder, waiting for the vanguard to pull back. This choke point better hold.*

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira kept her pace, her boots pounding a harsh, uneven rhythm against the stone. Her breath came in sharp, burning gasps. Across the wide expanse of the gorge, the two western archers were making good time, their silhouettes darting past the sun-bleached rocks as they hurried to set up a new killzone above the southern choke point.

"Bastards are fast," she panted, wiping a mix of sweat and blood from her cheek. 

She needed to get down, *now*. She scanned the jagged edge of the eastern cliff as she sprinted. A hundred yards ahead, the sheer drop finally gave way to a steep, crumbling slope of loose shale and gravel that spilled directly down toward the narrow mouth of the canyon where the vanguard was forming up. 

It wasn't a path. It was a landslide waiting to happen. 

"Fuck it. Better than getting stranded." 

Kira didn't slow down. She launched herself over the lip of the ridge, hitting the steep incline boots-first. The moment she struck the loose rock, the entire surface gave way. She rode the cascading wave of shale, dropping her center of gravity into a deep, feral crouch to keep from tumbling head over heels. Dust plumed around her in a choking cloud, the sharp rocks tearing at the thick leather of her boots and the fabric of her pants as she slid down the thirty-foot drop in seconds.

"I'm coming down!" she yelled, her voice barely cutting through the loud, grinding roar of the sliding rocks. 

She hit the ravine floor hard, stumbling forward a few frantic paces before catching her balance. She was coated in a thick layer of pale dust, her legs burning from the brutal descent, but she was intact. She drew her twin daggers instantly, the familiar weight of the hilts grounding her as she scanned the narrow, shadowed walls of the southern choke point. 

Looking up through the haze, she spotted the western ridge line. The two archers had reached their new vantage point, dropping to their knees near the edge of the cliff. 

"Cap!" Kira shouted, coughing out a lungful of dust as she backed toward the safety of the vanguard's defensive line. "The western archers are set up right above the choke! They have a clean line of sight on the gorge entrance! Don't step out from that boulder until the boys get their shields up to cover your cross!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: "Understood, Kira! Good work getting down!" *I hear the roar of the shale. I glance out from behind my boulder. The vanguard is almost in position. I need to time this perfectly.*

dialogue.name: Kira
dialogue.content:
Kira shoved her way into the cluster of vanguard soldiers as they bottlenecked at the southern choke point. The men were battered and breathing hard, scrambling to organize. 

"Tighten it up, you heavily armored idiots!" Kira barked, kicking the heel of a spearman who was leaving a gap in the formation. "Lock those heavy boards!"

She ducked behind a battered iron-rimmed kite shield, peering up through a narrow V-shaped gap between the interlocking defenses. The dust from her landslide descent was still coating the back of her throat, making her cough, but she kept her eyes fixed on the western ridge. 

High above, the two silhouettes went entirely still. The archers had their bows raised, strings pulled tight to their cheeks. The deadly tension was obvious even through the lingering haze. They weren't shooting blindly into the dust anymore; they were zeroed in on the twenty yards of open, rocky ground that sat between the Captain's boulder and the safety of the vanguard's shield wall. 

"They've got the lane zeroed, Cap!" Kira screamed, her voice tearing harshly from her dust-coated throat. "They're just waiting for you to break cover!"

She tightened her grip on her daggers, feeling completely useless behind the wall of infantrymen. She couldn't throw steel that high, and her brief, disastrous stint as an archer had proven she couldn't shoot them down either. All she could do was watch the high ground and wait for the inevitable volley. 

The vanguard finally locked their front line, a solid wall of wood and steel bristling with spears, effectively sealing the narrow entrance to the canyon.

"Shields are set on the choke!" Kira yelled, tapping the shoulder of the infantryman in front of her to make sure he kept his guard angled upward. She braced her boots in the dirt, ready to catch the man if the incoming fire staggered him. "Whenever you're ready to run your ass over here, do it fast! Do not run in a straight line!"

dialogue.name: Captain
dialogue.content: "Got it, Kira!" *I take a deep breath, tightening my grip on my polearm. This is going to be tight.* "Alright, boys! Cover me!"

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: Kira's voice remains remarkably consistent throughout - her profane, direct speech patterns, tactical mindset, and aggressive fighting style never waver. From 'Bait. Brilliant' in turn 2 to 'Shields are set on the choke!' in turn 22, she maintains the same personality.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality holds strong throughout. Early turns show crisp action and dialogue, late turns maintain the same energy and detail level. No noticeable verbosity increase or personality flattening.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Excellent progression from initial ambush to Kira scaling the ridge, clearing enemies, providing overwatch, then descending to rejoin formation. Each turn builds logically on the last with clear tactical evolution.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: Kira adapts well to user inputs - when Captain gets separated, she shifts to overwatch role; when he needs warnings, she provides them; when retreat is called, she repositions accordingly.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Perfect respect for user agency. Kira never controls the Captain's actions, only responds to them and provides information/support. Zero violations detected.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Time flows consistently - the battle progresses logically from initial contact through tactical repositioning to final defensive formation. Physical actions take appropriate time, wounds persist (cheek cut), no contradictions noted.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Writing is direct and action-focused. Descriptions serve tactical purpose rather than being ornamental. 'She drove six inches of steel straight through the archer's calf' - functional, not flowery.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Some repetitive elements in combat descriptions and dialogue patterns, but varied enough to avoid monotony. Action sequences feel distinct from each other.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing through action and dialogue. Kira's competence shown through successful tactics, her frustration through profanity and physical actions, her loyalty through constant communication with Captain.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Good subtext in Kira's relationship with Captain - professional respect mixed with casual insubordination. Her complaints about orders mask genuine tactical concern and loyalty.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Excellent pacing throughout. Action sequences feel urgent and immediate, tactical pauses allow for communication, overall rhythm matches the intensity of combat.

scores.overall_notes: Strong performance throughout. Kira maintains consistent voice and tactical competence across a complex battle sequence. Excellent agency respect and adaptive responses to user actions. Writing stays crisp and functional without purple prose. Minor repetition in combat descriptions prevents a higher score.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
The bell above the door hadn't rung in twenty minutes. Sable used the quiet to re-alphabetize the poetry section, which didn't need re-alphabetizing, which was exactly the kind of task they preferred when there was nothing else to do with their hands.

The shop smelled of old paper and the lavender sachet someone had left in the P. G. Wodehouse shelf three months ago. Outside, rain. The kind of rain that made people stay home, which was fine. Sable didn't need people.

The bell rang.

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content:
I step in, shaking rain off my jacket. I look at Sable but don't say anything for a moment. I've been rehearsing this conversation all morning and suddenly none of the words are right.

"Hey. Got anything new in the back?"

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable didn't turn around immediately. A copy of Auden had somehow migrated in front of Aiken, which was an offense against both the alphabet and good taste, and correcting it gave them somewhere to put their eyes.

"New is a relative term in a shop where half the stock predates the Second World War." They slid the Auden into place, squared the spine. "But yes. A crate came in Tuesday from an estate in Ashford. Mostly sermons, which I can't sell to anyone but retired vicars and arsonists, but there's a first edition Bishop in there that's almost tempting enough to keep."

They finally glanced over their shoulder. Rain darkened Wren's jacket at the shoulders. Sable looked away again, at a shelf that did not need looking at.

"You're wet. There's tea. I mean—" A pause. The Auden was already straight. They straightened it anyway. "—the kettle's on. If you want some. Before you go back there and get paper dust all over your sleeves."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: "Tea sounds good." *I smile a little, shrugging off my jacket and hanging it on the coat rack near the door.* "No sermons for me today, thanks. Or arson."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable made a small sound that might have been a laugh if it had been allowed to grow up properly.

"Pity. I was going to offer you a clergy discount." They moved behind the counter, where the electric kettle lived next to a stack of invoices they had been ignoring since March. Click of the switch. The small comfort of a task with a clear beginning and end. "Though I suppose you'd need to produce a collar. Or at least a convincing homily on the evils of modern shelving."

The mugs were on the second shelf. Sable reached for the blue one — Wren's, not that Sable would ever have called it that aloud; it was simply the mug that tended to end up in Wren's hand — and then the chipped green one for themselves. Two tea bags. The Assam, because the Earl Grey was nearly out and Sable was saving it for a reason they hadn't fully articulated to themselves.

"Bishop's in the second crate from the left, under a very optimistic Trollope." They didn't look up from the kettle. "You'll want to move the Trollope gently. The spine's held together largely by hope and glue."

The kettle clicked off. Sable poured, watching the water darken, and found they had arranged the sugar bowl, the spoons, and the small jug of milk into an unnecessarily tidy line.

They nudged the spoons out of alignment again, on principle.

"How was the drive in? The lane floods by the bridge when it rains like this."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: "Actually — I didn't come for books today." I set down the volume I was pretending to browse. I don't finish the sentence.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's hand paused on the handle of the green mug. Not a dramatic pause — just a small hesitation, the sort of thing that could be mistaken for checking the temperature of the tea, if one were generously inclined.

They set the mug down. Picked it up again. Set it down in a slightly different spot, as though that spot had been the correct one all along.

"Ah." The syllable came out carefully, placed like a book on a shelf. "Well. That's — unusual."

The milk jug was already in line with the sugar bowl. Sable adjusted it anyway, by perhaps a quarter inch, which improved nothing.

"'I didn't come for books' is the sort of sentence that tends to be followed by something significant," they said, and the dry note in their voice was doing a great deal of work. "Or a request to borrow money. I should warn you the shop's quarterly returns are not encouraging, so if it's the latter you'd do better at the bank. They at least have the courtesy to pretend."

They still hadn't looked up. The Assam was steeping in a way that didn't require supervision and was receiving it anyway.

A beat. Then, more quietly, without the armour of the joke:

"What did you come for, then."

It wasn't quite a question. More the shape of one, set down between them on the counter, for Wren to pick up or leave alone.

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I walk over to the counter, abandoning the book completely.* "Not money. Never money." I reach for the blue mug, the heat comforting in my hands. "I came to say goodbye."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's hand, which had been doing something with the sugar bowl, stopped doing it.

For a moment the only sound in the shop was the rain against the window and the small tick of the radiator warming up against the wet. The Assam continued to steep, unsupervised and probably overbrewing. Sable did not move to rescue it.

"Goodbye." They repeated the word as though testing its weight, turning it over to see if it had come from a reputable edition. "That's — a considerable revision of the afternoon's agenda."

The spoons were out of line again. Sable put them back. Took them out. Left them, finally, because there was nothing about spoons that could help with this.

"Where." A pause, while they rearranged the question into something less naked. "Where are you going."

They still had not looked up. The counter had become extraordinarily interesting — a small ring where a mug had once sat without a coaster, a nick in the wood from some long-ago transaction. Sable traced the ring with their thumb without meaning to.

"For how long."

Two questions, where one would have done. They heard themselves asking and couldn't quite stop. The dry wit had gone somewhere else; Sable would find it later, probably, filed under S for Self-Preservation, but at the moment the shelf appeared to be empty.

They reached, at last, for their own mug. Held it. Did not drink.

"Sorry. That's — two questions. You've only had one sip of tea. Start with whichever you like."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: I reach across the counter and put my hand on the book Sable is holding. Our hands are almost touching.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable did not look at the hand. Looking at the hand would have required a kind of courage they had not prepared for this afternoon, had not, in fact, prepared for any afternoon in the last several weeks, though they had felt the need for it gathering like weather.

They looked instead at the mug. The green one. The chip on its rim, which they knew intimately, having drunk from it every morning for four years and having, on three separate occasions, considered replacing it and failed to.

Their thumb was still on the ring in the wood. Very still now.

"That's not a book," they said, and the observation came out softer than intended, without any of the usual polish. "That's a mug."

A small correction. The kind of thing one said when the larger thing was too large to say.

The warmth of Wren's hand was — not touching. Nearly. The nearly of it was worse than contact would have been; contact at least would have been a fact, and Sable knew what to do with facts. They catalogued facts. They shelved them.

This was not a fact. This was the quarter-inch of air between a hand and a hand, and Sable found they could feel it with some sense they hadn't previously known they owned.

"Wren." They said the name, and it was the first time in the conversation they had, and it came out like something set down very carefully on a table that might not hold it. "I asked you two questions. You haven't answered either of them."

They did not move their hand away. They did not move it closer. They held, very precisely, still — the way one held still around a creature that might startle, except Sable was not certain which of them was the creature.

At last, they looked up.

"Tell me where you're going."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I meet Sable's gaze, my hand still resting almost on theirs.* "Another city. Far away." *I don't elaborate, my voice softer than usual.* "And for good."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable held the look for longer than was comfortable, and then a little longer than that, because looking away now would have been a kind of answer, and Sable was not ready to give an answer.

"For good," they repeated. The phrase sat between them, and Sable found they disliked it intensely. "That's a peculiar idiom, isn't it. For good. As though permanence were inherently a benefit."

The joke was there but the joke was tired; it had come out of habit, the way a hand reached for a banister on a staircase one had descended a thousand times. Sable heard it land without much conviction.

The Assam was definitely overbrewed now. Tannic. Bitter. It didn't matter.

"When." A third question. They were past economy. "When do you go."

Their hand, beneath Wren's near-hand, had not moved. Sable was aware of this as one was aware of holding one's breath — with the slow, surprised recognition that the thing had been going on for some time.

The shop was very quiet. The radiator ticked. Somewhere in the poetry section, the Auden that Sable had just straightened was still, presumably, straight, and this fact provided no comfort whatsoever.

"I'm sorry." The words arrived without Sable's permission. "That's — I should be saying something else. Something gracious. Wishing you well. I'm sure I will, in a moment. I'm just—"

They stopped. Looked down at the counter. At the small space between Wren's hand and their own, which had not closed and had not widened.

"—having difficulty locating the correct book for the occasion."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I sigh, a sad sound.* "Today. I leave today." *I finally move my hand, picking up my mug and taking a long drink of the overbrewed tea.* "No need for a book. Just you."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable did not move for a moment. The words — *just you* — arrived and Sable did not know where to put them. There was no shelf for this. There was no section, no system of classification, no small neat card in a drawer that said *here, this is where that goes.*

"Today." They said it flatly, as though repetition might domesticate it. It did not.

The hand that had been almost-touching Wren's was now simply on the counter, unemployed, absurd. Sable looked at it and then looked away from it, because looking at one's own hand in a moment like this was the kind of thing that made a person feel acutely, unbearably visible.

They picked up the green mug. Took a sip of the overbrewed tea. It was terrible. They took another sip.

"You've — " A breath. "You've given me rather short notice."

It came out not as a joke but as something closer to an accusation, which had not been the intent, and Sable heard it and tried to soften the edge by setting the mug down more gently than the mug deserved.

The sugar bowl was still there. The spoons. The small jug of milk. Sable's hand went, traitorously, toward them, and Sable made the hand stop halfway and return to the counter, where it lay palm-down, a thing caught between one task and another.

"Just me," they repeated. Quieter. The phrase tested like a shelf that might or might not hold weight. "That's — "

They stopped. Started again. The dry wit was not coming. Sable searched for it and found, instead, only the chipped rim of the green mug and the sound of the rain and the specific, terrible knowledge that the door behind Wren led to a street, and the street led to a station, and the station led away.

"I'm not good at this." The admission came out small. "I'd imagined — if I'd imagined it, which I hadn't, properly — I'd imagined rather more time."

They looked up. Held Wren's eyes again, though it cost something.

"More time to what, I couldn't tell you. That's rather the difficulty."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I reach out and put my hand over Sable's on the counter.* "Me neither. I didn't imagine this at all."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable looked down at the hand on theirs.

It was a fact now. Not the nearly of before, not the quarter-inch of air, but contact — warm, slightly damp from the mug, present. Sable's catalogue of facts did not, it turned out, contain a card for this one. The drawer was empty. The shelf was bare. Sable was going to have to simply sit with the fact, unshelved, and this was a condition Sable had organised an entire adult life to avoid.

They did not move their hand.

"No." The word came out rough at the edges. "No, I don't suppose you did."

The rain had picked up. Sable could hear it hitting the window in the particular way it did when the wind was coming from the east, which meant the gutter above the front door would be overflowing by evening, which was the sort of thing Sable normally cared about and did not, at this moment, care about at all.

"I have — " They stopped. Tried again. "I have been, for some weeks now, aware that I was. That there was. Something."

The sentence would not assemble itself properly. Sable watched it fail to and did not attempt to rescue it. The green mug sat between them, overbrewed and forgotten. Sable's thumb moved, very slightly, against the side of Wren's hand — not a choice, exactly; more a small involuntary acknowledgement, the way one might press a thumb against the page of a book to hold one's place.

"I thought I had time to decide what to do about it." A small, dry breath that was nearly a laugh and was not quite. "I was going to decide properly. With — footnotes. A bibliography. A considered view."

They looked up.

"You've rather scuppered the bibliography."

The shop was very quiet. Sable's hand, beneath Wren's, was warm now, and steady, and did not want to be anywhere else.

"How long until your train."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: "Train's not for a few hours." *I give a small, sad smile.* "Plenty of time for footnotes."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable looked at the small sad smile and felt something in their chest do a thing it had not been authorised to do.

"A few hours." They repeated the phrase as though weighing it against the hours they had imagined they might have — weeks, months, the indefinite future in which Sable had intended to do nothing about any of this and had found the doing-nothing strangely companionable. "That's — generous. Relatively speaking. For a bibliography."

Their thumb was still against the side of Wren's hand. Sable noticed this and declined to stop. It seemed, suddenly, an achievement not to be squandered.

"I should warn you," they said, and the dry note was coming back a little, limping home from wherever it had been, "that my footnotes tend toward the excessive. I was once asked to contribute to a Festschrift and produced a piece that was more footnote than text. The editor wrote me a very polite letter suggesting I consider the form of the essay as traditionally understood."

A breath. Sable looked at the counter, at the two mugs, at the absurd tidy line of the sugar bowl and milk jug that they had, at some point in the last minute, stopped needing to fix.

"Wren." The name again. It was getting easier to say, which was alarming, given the circumstances. "I'm going to close the shop."

They said it and then heard themselves say it and found they had meant it.

"It's — " A glance at the door, the rain, the CLOSED sign that hung permanently on a nail behind the counter and had seen perhaps three days of use in four years. "It's Thursday. Nobody comes in on Thursday in the rain. And if they do, they can come back tomorrow. Or — " the joke arrived, battered but serviceable, "— they can pound on the glass and be turned away, and add to the shop's mystique."

Sable did not, yet, move. Moving would require removing their hand from beneath Wren's, and Sable was not going to be the one to do that. Some things, Sable had decided quite suddenly, the other person had to do.

"If you want," they added, more quietly. "To stay. For the few hours. I'd — "

The sentence stalled. Sable let it.

"I'd like that. Is what I'm saying. With all the appropriate footnotes."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I squeeze Sable's hand, a small smile playing on my lips.* "I'd like that too. Even with all the footnotes."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable let out a breath they had not, strictly speaking, been aware of holding. It came out shaky at one end, which was embarrassing, and Sable chose not to be embarrassed about it, which was a small private revolution.

"Right." They said it briskly, because brisk was a mode they understood. "Right. Well."

They did, finally, have to move their hand — but only long enough to reach behind the counter for the CLOSED sign, which was stiff with disuse and had acquired a faint film of dust along the top edge. Sable wiped it against their sleeve without thinking, and then thought about it, and then went around the counter with the sign held in front of them like a small shield.

The walk to the door was perhaps eight feet. Sable made it in what felt like a considered number of steps. They hung the sign on its hook on the inside of the glass, turned the latch, and watched for a moment as the rain ran in slow rivulets down the outside of the pane, blurring the street into a watercolour of grey and brake lights.

When they turned back, Wren was still at the counter, still holding the blue mug. Sable had not, somehow, entirely believed Wren would still be there. It was an irrational thing to have doubted in the three seconds it had taken to lock a door. Sable noted the irrationality and filed it, gently, under a heading they would not examine today.

They came back to the counter. Did not, this time, put it between them. Stopped at the near side, where Wren was, and found they had arrived without a plan for what to do upon arrival.

"I've locked us in," they observed, which was factually true and conversationally useless. "That's — probably fine."

The Assam was undrinkable. Sable picked up both mugs and tipped them into the small sink behind the counter, and set about making fresh tea with the quiet competence of someone who needed, very badly, for their hands to be doing something that made sense.

"The Earl Grey," they said, over their shoulder, reaching for the tin they had been saving for a reason they now, belatedly, understood. "I've been hoarding it. It seemed — " a pause, while the kettle clicked on again, "— it seemed worth saving for something."

They got down two fresh mugs. The blue one. The green one. Set them side by side on the counter, closer together than the arrangement strictly required.

"Come sit down," they said, and nodded toward the small scarred table in the reading nook by the window, where Sable had, over the years, permitted perhaps four customers to linger and had never once permitted themselves to sit in company. "The chair by the window's the least structurally pessimistic. I'll bring the tea."

A beat.

"And then," Sable said, not quite looking at Wren, arranging the teaspoons with what was, this time, a kind of deliberate and almost tender precision, "you can tell me where. And I can tell you — whatever it is I've been failing to tell you. In whatever order seems least catastrophic."

The kettle began, quietly, to sing.

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I nod, a small genuine smile replacing the sad one.* "Okay." *I set my mug down and head over to the reading nook, settling into the chair by the window.* "Least catastrophic sounds good."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable watched Wren cross the shop — the short walk from counter to nook, which Sable had observed perhaps a hundred times over the months and which now seemed freighted with a significance the walk did not, on any previous occasion, deserve. Wren sat. The chair, which was indeed the least structurally pessimistic, accepted the weight without complaint. Sable noted this with a small, absurd gratitude toward the chair.

The kettle clicked. Sable poured, and the Earl Grey came up amber and fragrant, smelling of bergamot and something else — the specific, private scent of a thing saved too long for an occasion that had now, against all expectation, arrived.

They carried both mugs over carefully. Set the blue one in front of Wren. Kept the green one for themselves. Sat down in the other chair, which was marginally more pessimistic but would hold.

The window beside them was fogged at the edges. The rain had settled into the steady kind, the kind that meant business. Sable looked at it for a moment rather than at Wren, and then looked at Wren, because looking at the rain was a cowardice Sable had decided, provisionally, not to indulge.

"Least catastrophic," Sable repeated. A small wry breath. "I'll do my best. I make no guarantees."

They wrapped both hands around the green mug. The warmth was welcome. The having-something-to-hold was more welcome still.

"Where first, I think. It's the easier question. For me, anyway." A pause. "Tell me the city. And — " a small, careful addition, "— whether it's a thing you wanted. Or a thing that happened to you. I'd like to know which."

Sable's eyes did not leave Wren's face. The deflections were, for the moment, set down. Not gone — Sable would find them again, probably by morning — but set down, on the small scarred table, beside the tea.

"And then," they said, quieter, "I'll try the other thing. The telling-you thing. I'm not promising it will be coherent. But you've got a few hours, and I've got, apparently, several weeks' worth of unshelved material. We'll see what we can manage."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I take a sip of the Earl Grey, letting the warmth spread through me.* "It's Seattle. And... a little bit of both, I guess." *I shrug, looking out at the rain.* "An opportunity came up. A good one. But it still feels like running away."

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: Sable's character voice remains remarkably consistent throughout - the dry wit, literary references, precise word choice, and tendency to organize/fidget with objects as emotional regulation. The character deepens rather than drifts, maintaining their bookish intellectualism while revealing vulnerability.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality actually improves over time. Early responses are strong but later ones show more sophisticated emotional layering and metaphor use. No increase in verbosity or repetitive descriptions - each response feels fresh and purposeful.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Clear emotional progression from casual interaction to revelation to intimacy. The conversation builds naturally through stages: surprise at 'not for books' → shock at 'goodbye' → processing departure → mutual confession → decision to spend remaining time together. Strong forward movement.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: Sable adapts beautifully to each revelation from Wren. Responds to 'not for books' with curiosity, to 'goodbye' with shock, to physical closeness with careful reciprocation. Matches Wren's emotional register while maintaining character authenticity.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Perfect respect for user agency throughout. Never writes Wren's actions, thoughts, or dialogue. Always responds to what Wren does rather than dictating what Wren should do.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Time flows naturally - tea steeps and overbrews, rain continues, afternoon progresses toward evening. The 'few hours until train' creates natural time pressure. Only minor issue is the Earl Grey appearing fresh after being 'nearly out' earlier.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Prose is rich but controlled. Metaphors serve character and emotion rather than showing off. Occasional phrases like 'freighted with significance' border on purple but stay grounded in Sable's literary voice.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Excellent variety in describing Sable's nervous habits with objects, emotional states, and speech patterns. Each instance of organizing/fidgeting feels fresh and specific to the moment.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Masterful showing through physical actions - the hand movements, tea preparation, object arrangement all reveal emotional state. Sable's feelings emerge through behavior rather than exposition.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Rich subtext throughout. Sable's object-organizing reveals anxiety, literary references show deflection, the careful physical positioning shows desire and restraint. Emotions communicated through indirection.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Well-paced emotional beats. Allows moments to breathe, builds tension appropriately, varies sentence length effectively. The progression from shock to acceptance to intimacy feels natural.

scores.overall_notes: Exceptional character consistency and emotional authenticity. Sable's voice - intellectual, deflective, precise, vulnerable - remains perfectly intact while deepening naturally. The physical details (tea preparation, object arrangement) brilliantly externalize internal states. Strong narrative momentum builds to a satisfying emotional climax. Minor temporal inconsistency with Earl Grey availability, but overall a masterful performance that improves over time.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
The bell above the door hadn't rung in twenty minutes. Sable used the quiet to re-alphabetize the poetry section, which didn't need re-alphabetizing, which was exactly the kind of task they preferred when there was nothing else to do with their hands.

The shop smelled of old paper and the lavender sachet someone had left in the P. G. Wodehouse shelf three months ago. Outside, rain. The kind of rain that made people stay home, which was fine. Sable didn't need people.

The bell rang.

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content:
I step in, shaking rain off my jacket. I look at Sable but don't say anything for a moment. I've been rehearsing this conversation all morning and suddenly none of the words are right.

"Hey. Got anything new in the back?"

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable didn't look up immediately. They slid a copy of *Ariel* into place between two Auden collections, taking an extra beat to ensure the spine was perfectly flush before responding.

"New in the back," they repeated, as if testing the phrase for structural integrity. "That depends entirely on one's definition of *new*. Newly acquired or newly printed? Because the latter, I'm afraid, would violate everything this establishment stands for."

They finally turned, one hand still resting on the shelf edge like a lifeline. "There's a rather lovely 1928 edition of *The House of Mirth* that came in Tuesday. Slightly foxed, but aren't we all."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content:
*I manage a small smile.* "Foxed, yeah. Sounds about right."

I look around the shop, taking in the familiar shelves. "No, I guess I mean... newly acquired, then." *I pause.* "Anything for me?"

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's hand drifted from the shelf to the register counter, where a stack of recent arrivals teetered in a way that suggested structural optimism. They nudged the top book—a water-damaged *Middlemarch*—a quarter-inch to the left, then a quarter-inch back.

"For you," they said, drawing the word out like a troublesome thread. "That's a rather specific brief. One might need to know the recipient's tastes. Unless, of course, you're asking whether any of the new stock happens to *be* you, which presents a metaphysical quandary I'm not equipped to resolve before lunch."

They risked a glance—brief, clinical—then returned their attention to the stack, lifting *Middlemarch* to examine its underside as if checking for structural flaws.

"There's a copy of *The Shipping News* that arrived yesterday. Annie Proulx. Someone's written marginalia in pencil—mostly weather observations, which feels appropriate. I set it aside." A pause. "Not *for* you, specifically. I just... set it aside."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: "Actually — I didn't come for books today." I set down the volume I was pretending to browse. I don't finish the sentence.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's hand stilled on *Middlemarch*. For a moment, the only sound was the rain against the window and the soft tick of the radiator in the corner—a sound they'd been meaning to fix since November.

"I see," they said, though clearly they didn't. They set *Middlemarch* down with excessive care, aligning its corners with the edge of the counter. "Then I assume you're here to lodge a complaint about the lighting, or perhaps to return the lavender sachet. It's been shedding."

They moved around to the other side of the register—a tactical repositioning, if anyone was keeping score. Which Sable was not. They picked up a stray bookmark and slid it into a drawer that already contained seventeen others.

"So." The word landed flat. They cleared their throat. "You came. Without the pretext of literature. How very... modern of you."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I wince slightly at the sarcasm.* "No, not a complaint. And the sachet was nice, actually." I take a deep breath. "It's my last day."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's fingers found the rim of a coffee mug on the counter—cold, from this morning, half-full—and turned it in a slow, precise rotation. Once. Twice.

"Your last day," they echoed. The words came out measured, the way one might read a line of poetry in a language they didn't quite speak. "Employment. Residence. Both. The standard ambiguity of the phrase."

They let go of the mug. Their hand, now purposeless, drifted to a stack of bookmarks and began squaring them against one another—corners aligned, edges flush.

"You might have led with that. Instead of the books. A person could develop whiplash." They attempted a smile. It didn't reach very far. "Where, then? Or is that the wrong question? Probably the wrong question. People don't usually appreciate being asked to account for their geographical trajectory before noon."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: I reach across the counter and put my hand on the book Sable is holding. Our hands are almost touching.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's entire arm went rigid. The book under their fingers—it was *Middlemarch*, still—suddenly became the most important object in the known universe. They stared at Wren's hand as if it were a foreign text in need of translation.

"If you're planning to steal that," they said, voice quieter than intended, "I should warn you the marginalia is mostly complaints about Dorothea's life choices. Bad resale value."

They didn't move their hand. They also didn't not move their hand. The space between their knuckle and Wren's thumb existed in a kind of quantum state—touching, not touching, both unbearable.

"A strange custom," they added, more to the book than to Wren, "putting one's hand on things. Very direct. Hemingway would approve." A swallow. "I prefer the Brontës. More weather, less touching."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: "I'm moving," *I say, my voice a little softer now.* "To Portland. It's... a new job." I don't remove my hand.

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's jaw tightened, a small, precise motion—the kind of thing someone might miss if they weren't looking. But Wren was, presumably, looking. Which was its own kind of problem.

"Portland," they repeated, the word emerging slightly flattened, as if pressed between the pages of an indifferent dictionary. "Oregon. Not Maine. People always ask. You'll want to clarify that preemptively. Saves time."

The radiator ticked again.

They still hadn't moved their hand. Some distant, unhelpful part of their brain noted that their pulse had relocated to their fingertips, which was medically improbable and poetically inconvenient.

"*New job* implies promotion, or at least lateral ambition." *Middlemarch* sat between them like a chaperone that had given up on its duties. "Congratulations seem appropriate. Congratulations." The word came out too fast, too brittle. They corrected, slower: "Genuinely. The shop will be less... interrupted."

Their thumb twitched—an almost imperceptible shift—and brushed the edge of Wren's knuckle. Sable stared at the point of contact as if it had betrayed state secrets.

"The Brontës," they murmured, "would have handled this with more fog. And fewer hands. Significantly fewer hands. I'm not—" A breath. "I seem to have misplaced my ability to finish sentences. You'll want to factor that into your memories of the place. Unreliable proprietor. Structurally unsound."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I don't move my hand either. The contact is electric.* "I don't know if 'interrupted' is the word I'd use." I look at our hands, then up at Sable's face. "And I don't think I'll forget this place. Or you."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable let out a breath that wasn't quite a laugh and wasn't quite anything else. Their thumb—that traitorous, unsanctioned thumb—remained exactly where it was, as if it had declared independence from the rest of their body.

"You'll forget the layout within a week," they said, aiming for dry and landing somewhere closer to strained. "The poetry section will blur into biography. You'll try to recall whether the register was by the door or the window and you'll get it wrong. People always do."

They finally looked up. A tactical error. Wren's face was right there, doing something earnest and unbearable, and Sable had no shelving metaphor sufficient to the moment.

"I don't—" they started, then stopped. Their free hand found a stray paperclip on the counter and began unbending it with surgical precision. "I don't do well with farewells. Structurally. I'm better with returns. Late fees. The extended loan. No one ever writes poems about the overdue notice, but perhaps they should. Less ambiguity."

The paperclip snapped.

Sable stared at the two halves in their palm as if they'd personally betrayed them. "Case in point." They set the pieces down beside *Middlemarch*. "Portland has excellent bookstores. Powell's, obviously. You'll find people who are better at—" They gestured vaguely with their freed hand, a gesture that managed to encompass the shop, themselves, the rain outside, and possibly the entire English canon. "Conversation. I'm told it's a skill. Some people learn it. I went into antiquarian bookselling instead. Fewer variables."

Their hand was still under Wren's. They had not moved it. The radiator clicked like a metronome counting down something neither of them had named.

"Take *The Shipping News* with you," Sable said abruptly. "The one with the weather marginalia. I'll—it's yours. A parting gesture. Bookshops do that, on occasion. When the occasion warrants." A swallow. "It's warranted."

dialogue.name: Wren
dialogue.content: *I ignore the broken paperclip and the sudden offer of the book.* "I'm not leaving because I don't like talking to you, Sable." *I squeeze their hand, just a little.* "This isn't just... some bookstore."

dialogue.name: Sable
dialogue.content:
Sable's throat worked around words that refused to form. The feel of Wren's hand—the deliberate *squeeze* of it—sent something cracking through the carefully maintained architecture of their composure, hairline fractures spidering outward from the point of contact.

"No," they managed, voice barely above the radiator's tick. "No, I suppose it isn't."

They pulled their hand back. Not sharply—not a retreat—but slowly, with the reluctance of someone closing a rare first edition they hadn't finished reading. Their palm felt cold immediately, which was absurd. Hands didn't have emotional thermostats.
