Your free hand drifts, almost of its own accord, to the small of your back. The pistol is there. Suppressed. Eight in the magazine, one in the chamber. A stairwell is a terrible place for a gunfight — the acoustics alone will tell every operative in the building exactly which shaft you're in — but a suppressed round into the stairwell lighting on a lower landing would cost you maybe two seconds of noise and buy you a dark spiral they'd have to clear inch by inch.

Mira is watching your hand. She knows what you're thinking. She doesn't say anything. She's letting you choose.

The boots hit floor five.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I pull Mira up, my hand finding the small of my back. "Stay here," I tell her, low and urgent. *A suppressed shot into a light source*. I take a breath, aim, and fire.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

You ease Mira down against the inside wall of the landing, where the concrete lip hides her from anyone looking up through the central shaft. Her hand finds your wrist — brief, hard, a grip that says *don't be long* — and then lets go.

You step to the rail.

You don't lean over. You lean *through* — just enough to sight down the spiral, one eye past the edge, the pistol coming up in a smooth two-handed bracket. The stairwell drops away beneath you in concentric rectangles of amber, each landing lit by a single industrial fixture bolted to the underside of the flight above. You count down. Floor eight (yours, dark-sided). Floor seven. Floor six. Floor five — and there, the light directly above where the boots are about to round.

You exhale half a breath and hold.

The pistol coughs.

The sound is not silent — nothing suppressed ever is — but it's a flat, wet *thup* that the stairwell swallows and smears. The fixture on floor five bursts in a spit of glass and filament. The amber on that landing dies. A heartbeat later the fixture on floor four goes the same way — you've already shifted aim, already fired again, because one dark landing is a problem and two dark landings in a row is a *blind corridor*. Glass rains down the shaft in a thin, musical patter.

Below, the boots stop.

A voice barks. Not a question this time — an order. The radio squawks back at it, overlapping. You hear the distinct metallic *snick* of a weapon being brought up to ready. They know. They know exactly what just happened and exactly what it means: someone above them has a suppressor and the presence of mind to kill the lights instead of the men. That is not a panicking defector. That is a professional telling them the next ten meters cost blood.

They will still come. But not fast. Not now.

You holster in one motion and you're back to Mira before she's finished the breath she started when you fired. Her eyes are closed. For a sickening half-second you think she's gone under — then they open, and they are still hers, still sharp, still counting.

"Two," she whispers. "You fired two."

"Lights. Not people."

Something in her face eases a fraction. You file that away — she noticed, she cared which — and you get your shoulder back under her arm.

"Seven floors," you tell her. "They're slow now. We go."

She nods, and you move.

Floor nine passes in a haze of her ragged breathing and the scrape of her leg. Floor ten, her head lolls once against your shoulder and you shake her — roughly, because gentle won't reach her anymore — and she comes back with a gasp and a muttered word in a language you don't speak. Floor eleven, you hear, far below and muffled by distance and concrete, the first careful footfall resuming. They're climbing again. Slower. Weapons up. Clearing each dark landing before committing to the next.

You've bought yourself, maybe, four minutes.

Floor twelve. The stairwell ends. A heavy steel door, painted the same industrial grey as everything else, stencilled in faded Cyrillic: ROOF ACCESS — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL. A card reader glows a steady, unbothered red beside the handle.

Mira sags against the wall. Her face is the colour of old paper. Blood has reached her boot; you can see the dark shine of it on the leather now, and you finally, against your own rule, look.

You shouldn't have looked.

Her thigh, high and inside, is soaked through. Not arterial — she'd already be dead — but venous, steady, the kind of bleed that has been patiently emptying her into your shirt for the last eleven floors. She sees you see it.

"I know," she says quietly.

You turn to the card reader. No card. No time. Eleven minutes to a car you haven't reached. Four minutes to men who are climbing toward you in the dark.

The door has a hinge side and a lock side and a seam you can read.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I set Mira down in the server room and barricade the door. I need to think.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

Wait.

You blink. Your hand is on the roof-access door, your other arm is around Mira's ribs, and for a moment your own mind has skipped a track — *server room*, you thought, *barricade*, as if there were a door behind you that wasn't a twelve-story drop down a stairwell full of men with rifles. There is no server room. There is this landing, this door, this woman bleeding out against your side, and the amber light flickering because somewhere in the building the alarm system is still fighting itself.

You shake it off. Exhaustion is a liar and you don't have time for its stories.

The door. Focus.

You lower Mira down the wall beside the hinge, where she'll be out of any spall line if you have to do this loud. Her head tips back against the concrete. Her eyes stay open, which is what matters. You crouch in front of her, one hand on her uninjured knee.

"Mira. Listen to me. Your belt."

She looks down at herself, then at you, and her hands — slow, shaking, but obedient — go to the buckle. You help her. You thread the leather free of the loops in three quick pulls and you cinch it high on her thigh, above the soak, above where you think the bleed is coming from. You pull it until she makes a sound and then you pull it one notch further and lock it. Her head rolls. Her breath goes shallow and fast.

"That buys you time," you tell her. "Not a lot. Some."

"Door," she whispers.

"Door," you agree.

You stand. You turn to it. The card reader's red eye watches you, patient and stupid. You ignore it. Card readers are a conversation you don't have the vocabulary for, not in four minutes, not without tools. But the door itself — the door is just metal and mechanism, and every mechanism has a weakness.

You check the hinges. Interior-side. Bad for kicking, good for you: it means the door opens inward, toward you, which means the strike plate and the deadbolt are what's holding it, not the hinge pins. You check the seam at the latch. Standard commercial deadbolt, probably a single-cylinder tied to the card reader's solenoid. The frame around it is the original build — painted-over steel, but steel, not reinforced. Whoever retrofitted the card system did it cheap.

You draw the pistol again.

Six rounds left. You can spare one. You'd rather spare none, but you'd rather spare none of your remaining minutes either, and a bullet is faster than a pry.

You press the muzzle against the seam just above the deadbolt — angled down, into the bolt itself, not the reader — and you turn your face away and fire.

The *thup* is louder this time because the barrel is against metal and the metal sings. The bolt shears. Something inside the door clatters. You hit the door with your shoulder and it gives — not all the way, the frame bites back — and you hit it again and it swings inward with a grinding protest and cold air hits your face.

Night. Wind. The low wet smell of a city after rain.

You're on the roof.

You grab Mira under the arms and haul her through the threshold. Her boots drag. You pull the door shut behind you — it doesn't latch anymore, the bolt is gone, but it sits closed, and that will cost the men below a precious second of confusion when they reach it and find it dark and silent and not locked.

The roof stretches out around you in tar and gravel and the humped silhouettes of HVAC units. The sky is low and orange with the city's light. Somewhere to the north, the telecom building rises — you can see its red aviation beacon pulsing against the cloud. Between you and it, invisible from here, is a gantry that doesn't officially exist.

Mira is a dead weight against your side now. The tourniquet is doing its job on the bleed but it isn't doing anything for the litres she's already lost. Her lips are moving. You bend your ear to her.

"…said *package*," she breathes. "He said *flagged*."

"I heard him."

"You should know what it is." Her hand fumbles at her own collar, at the seam of her jacket lining. "Before we cross. In case I don't."

Her fingers aren't working. You'll have to do it for her, if you want to know. Or you can not know, and get her across, and let the eleven-minute car and whoever's running this pick the secret apart on the other side.

Behind and below you, muffled through twelve floors of concrete, you think you hear the first dark landing being cleared. Or you imagine it. The distance and the wind make liars of your ears.

The northwest corner is maybe forty meters away.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *Damn it.* "No time," I tell her, my voice urgent. Her fingers are fumbling, useless. I grab her jacket. "Hold still." I need to see what this "package" is.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

You drop to one knee in the gravel and take her jacket in both hands. She doesn't resist — she couldn't, now, even if she wanted to. Her head tips back against your forearm and her eyes track the orange sky above you like she's looking for a star she used to know.

The lining. Her fingers were at the collar seam. You feel along it with your thumb, quick, professional, and you find it almost immediately: a ridge where there shouldn't be one, a stiffness in the fabric that doesn't match the weave around it. Someone sewed this in a hurry. Someone sewed this in the last twenty-four hours.

You don't have a knife in your hand and you're not going to waste the seconds drawing one. You hook your thumbnail under the stitching and you tear. The thread gives in a ragged line and something hard slides against your fingers.

You pull it free.

It sits in your palm smaller than a coin. A matte black rectangle, maybe fifteen millimeters by twenty, with a single notch at one edge and a contact pad on the underside. A chip. Not a drive, not a card — a chip, the kind of thing that goes *inside* another piece of hardware. You've seen their cousins before, in briefings you half-listened to because the technical people were talking. Cryptographic key module. Or a key *seed*. Something that doesn't carry data so much as it carries the authority to unlock data that lives somewhere else.

Flagged. *Flagged* means every signals-intelligence desk from here to the Urals has a standing order about this object. *Flagged* means metal detectors, millimeter scanners, airport rails, border kiosks — and it means the men climbing the stairwell behind you are not trying to recover Mira. They are trying to recover *this*, and Mira is acceptable collateral either way.

Your handler knew. Your handler told you and then left the call to you, which is a thing handlers do when they already know what they want the answer to be and they don't want it on their own ledger.

You close your fist around the chip.

Mira's eyes find yours. She's watching your face and she's reading it — she was their analyst, she reads faces for breakfast — and whatever she sees there makes her exhale a long, slow breath that sounds almost like relief.

"Good," she whispers. "You understand now."

"I understand enough."

"Not…" Her tongue is thick. She tries again. "Not just me they want. So if you have to choose—"

"I'm not choosing." You pocket the chip — inside pocket, against your ribs, where a body search would find it but a grab-and-run wouldn't. You get your arm back under hers. "I'm crossing the gantry with you. Both of us. That's the plan."

"Plans," she says, and almost smiles.

You haul her upright. The tourniquet has darkened the leather where it bites her thigh. Her good leg takes weight, barely. The bad one is a passenger now.

Forty meters of tar and gravel. The HVAC units make a broken maze of it — three big ones humped between you and the northwest corner, conduit snaking between them, a low parapet running the roof's edge. Cover, if you need it. Obstacles, if you don't.

You move. Her boots scrape gravel. The wind up here is stronger than you expected and it pulls at your wet shirt where her blood has soaked through; the cold of it hits your skin and you realize, distantly, that you are shaking. Not from fear. From the long adrenaline tail of twelve floors and a suppressed shot into the dark and a decision you just made about a woman's life and a chip in your pocket.

You pass the first HVAC unit. The second. The northwest corner resolves itself out of the roof's geometry — a slight notch in the parapet, a bolted plate in the tar, and, when you reach it and look over the edge, the gantry.

Your handler wasn't lying. It's there.

It's also worse than you hoped.

A narrow steel catwalk, maybe sixty centimeters wide, with a single handrail on the left side only. It spans — you measure by eye — something like eighteen meters of open air to the roof of the telecom building, which sits a full story lower than yours. The gantry slopes down to meet it. The far end disappears into a small housing with a hatch on top. Six years of weather have done what six years of weather do: the decking is streaked with rust, the handrail's paint is blistered, and one of the support cables on the underside sags in a way you don't love.

Below the gantry is nothing. Twelve stories of nothing, and then an alley, and then the wet shine of Kuznetsova Street catching the streetlights.

Mira looks at it. She looks at you.

"I can't walk that," she says, flat. Not despair. Just information.

Behind you, across the roof, the stairwell door you came through grinds faintly. Not open — not yet. Someone on the other side has reached it and is testing what they're dealing with. The bolt you shot is gone but the door is heavy and it sits in its frame, and the men behind it don't yet know whether the roof is empty or whether a suppressor is waiting for the first face through.

They'll know in about thirty seconds.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I look from Mira to the gantry, then back to the door. "You don't have to walk it. Just hold on." I start pulling her towards the gantry.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

You get both arms under her — one across her back, one hooked beneath her knees — and you lift. She's lighter than she should be, or you're running hotter than you should be, or both. Her head finds the hollow of your shoulder and stays there. Her arm loops around your neck, weak but willing. Her good hand fists in the collar of your jacket.

"Hold on," you tell her. "Whatever happens. Hold on."

"Holding," she breathes.

You step over the parapet.

The gantry meets your boot with a hollow metallic *thunk* that carries further than you like. The whole structure shifts — a small, sickening give, the kind of movement that tells you the bolts at this end are still doing their job but are not enthusiastic about it. You freeze for half a heartbeat, testing. It holds.

You commit.

The trick is not to look down, and the trick is also not to *not* look down — you have to watch the decking, because six years of weather means six years of corrosion and you do not want to find the one panel that's rusted through by standing on it. You read the walkway the way you'd read a minefield: bright rust good, dark rust worse, actual holes worst. You pick your line.

One step. Two. The wind hits you broadside and Mira's weight shifts against your chest and for one vertiginous second the gantry tilts in your perception even though it hasn't moved. You widen your stance. You find the rail with your left elbow — can't grip it, your hands are full of woman — and you press your forearm against it for a third point of contact.

Three steps. Four. You are now over open air.

Behind you, the stairwell door grinds again — louder — and then with a scrape of protest it gives. You hear it even over the wind. You hear it because part of you has been listening for nothing else.

You don't look back. Looking back costs balance and balance is the only currency you have left.

Five steps. Six. The slope of the gantry begins its gentle descent toward the telecom roof and gravity becomes a small, grateful friend — each step now wants to happen, rather than having to be forced. Mira's breath is shallow against your throat. Her fingers in your collar have gone slack. You shake your shoulder, hard.

"Mira. Hold."

"…holding."

Seven. Eight. Halfway.

A voice behind you. Russian, or close. Not shouting — *calling*. The kind of call a professional makes when he's seen a target and he doesn't want to waste the shot by startling it into cover. You don't speak the language but you know that tone in any tongue. It means *stop and we don't have to*.

You do not stop.

A beat. Then the sound you were expecting and dreading in equal measure: the flat crack of an unsuppressed rifle, open air, no walls to contain it. The round goes somewhere — not into you, not into Mira, not into the gantry under your feet. A warning, or a miss, or a ranging shot. You don't get to know which. You get to keep walking.

Nine. Ten. The gantry's support cable — the one that sagged — groans somewhere beneath you, a long low complaint of stressed metal, and you feel the decking flex under your weight in a way it didn't a moment ago. You do not run. Running on this would be the last decision you ever made. You lengthen your stride instead, smooth and even, and you count panels.

A second crack behind you. This one hits — not you — the handrail, two meters ahead, a bright spark of torn paint and deflected metal. They're ranging now. The next one will be closer.

Eleven. Twelve. The telecom roof rises to meet you, a flat black expanse with the little housing and its hatch squatting at the gantry's end like a promise. Five meters. Four.

A third crack. This one you feel — a hot line across the outside of your left thigh, a hard tug like someone caught you with a hook and let go. Your leg answers, which means it still works, which is all you're asking of it right now. Mira makes a small sound against your neck; you don't know if she felt it or if she's just surfacing.

Three meters. Two.

You step off the gantry onto the telecom roof and the solid stillness of it under your boots is so shocking after the swaying catwalk that you almost go down on the transition. You don't. You stagger two paces, three, and put the bulk of the housing between your body and the stairwell roof you just crossed from. Cover. Real cover. Concrete and steel and eighteen meters of empty air between you and the men with rifles.

You drop to one knee behind the housing. You lower Mira as gently as the shaking in your arms will allow. Her back finds the wall. Her head lolls, catches, steadies.

The hatch is right above you. A simple drop-hatch with an interior ladder, the kind that opens from either side. No card reader up here. No one planned for anyone to ever need this.

Your thigh is warm and wet in a narrow stripe down the outside. You look. A graze — long, shallow, bleeding freely but not pumping. You've had worse from barbed wire. You file it.

Your earpiece clicks.

"Ghost." Your handler. Alive, still on the line, and audibly breathing harder than he was. "I saw your beacon cross. Telecom stairwell is clean to the lobby. Car is eight minutes. *Eight.* Do not exit before eight — I say again, do not exit early, the street is not clear until eight."

Eight minutes. In a stairwell. With a woman who has the complexion of wet chalk and a tourniquet that is the only thing keeping her in the conversation.

Across the gap, on the roof you came from, you hear boots on gravel. They've reached the parapet. They're looking at the gantry. They're making the same calculation you just made, in reverse: can they cross, should they cross, is it worth the eighteen meters of exposure. At least one of them will try. Maybe not all of them — some will peel off to find stairs, elevators, the long way around to the telecom lobby, and *those* are the ones your handler's eight-minute window has to outrun.

The chip is a small hard weight against your ribs. Mira's eyes are half-closed. The hatch waits above you.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "Eight minutes," I grunt to Mira, pushing myself up. "Can you make it down a ladder?" I don't wait for an answer. *Time to go.* I reach for the hatch.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

You reach up and grip the hatch's recessed handle. It's stiff — six years of weather has welded it into a suggestion rather than a mechanism — and for a bad second it doesn't move. You reset your grip, brace your good leg against the housing wall, and wrench.

It gives with a rusted shriek that you feel in your teeth. The hatch lifts, catches on its stay, and stays open. A square of dim fluorescent light spills up out of the opening. Warm air rises past your face — building air, dry, electrical, the particular ozone smell of a telecom facility running hot.

Below: a steel ladder, painted the same bored grey as everything in this line of work, descending maybe three meters into a small mechanical space. Beyond that space, through an open doorway, you can see the top landing of a stairwell. Clean. Lit. Empty.

"Mira." You crouch back to her. Her eyes find yours — slower this time, taking a half-second longer than they did at the gantry's near end. The tourniquet is holding but her body is running out of the thing tourniquets can't replace. "Ladder. Three meters. I go first, I catch you. You don't climb — you drop. Understand?"

"Drop," she echoes.

"Good."

Across the gap, something heavy rings against metal. You glance — one glance, permitted — around the edge of the housing. A figure is on the gantry. Only one, so far. He's moving in a low crouch, rifle slung across his back to free his hands for the handrail, and he's smart about it; he's reading the decking the same way you did. He's maybe four panels in. He has fourteen to go.

A second figure waits at the parapet, rifle up, covering him. *That's* the one who'll shoot you if you show a silhouette for longer than a breath.

You duck back. You don't have fourteen panels of time. You have however long it takes that crouched man to decide the gantry won't kill him faster than you will.

You swing your legs into the hatch. The ladder's rungs bite your palms — cold, flaking paint, solid underneath. You descend three rungs fast, get your head below the lip of the opening, and then you stop and brace: feet wide on a rung, one arm hooked through the ladder, the other reaching up.

"Mira. Now."

She uses the wall. She gets a hand on the hatch's rim — her good hand, white-knuckled — and she drags herself to the opening more than she climbs to it. Her legs won't help her. Her bad leg is a piece of furniture she's carrying. She gets her hips over the edge and then gravity takes the decision away from both of you and she *falls*.

You catch her.

Not gracefully. Her weight slams into your chest and your braced arm and for one bad moment the ladder and your shoulder and her ribs all make competing claims on physics, and then you absorb her, pinning her against the ladder with your body, her face against your collarbone, your arm locked through the rungs behind her back. The ladder rings once, hard, a bell-note of protest. Your left thigh — the grazed one — screams where her weight drives against it.

It holds. You hold.

"Reach up," you grunt. "The hatch. Pull it shut."

Her good arm rises, trembling, and finds the underside of the hatch. She pulls. It swings down — stiff, slow, then faster as gravity takes it — and slams closed above you with a flat metallic *boom* that echoes down the ladder shaft.

Dark, for a second. Then your eyes adjust to the fluorescent bleed from the doorway below and the world comes back in shades of institutional green.

You descend. One rung at a time, Mira pinned between you and the ladder, her feet riding on top of yours on each rung — a trick you learned somewhere you no longer remember, drunk probably, with someone who is probably dead now. It works. It always works.

Your boots hit concrete.

You carry her the three steps through the doorway, into the top landing of the telecom stairwell, and you lower her against the wall. The stairwell is warmer than the one you just left — better insulated, better maintained, the light a clean white instead of emergency amber. It smells of floor polish and copper wiring. It smells, absurdly, normal.

You check your watch. Six minutes, forty seconds until the car.

You check Mira. Her lips are moving without sound. Her eyes are open but not tracking. You put two fingers against her throat and find a pulse — fast, thready, the kind of pulse that tells you her body is negotiating with itself and losing the negotiation. The tourniquet has done what tourniquets do. It has not done what transfusions do.

Your earpiece clicks.

"Ghost. Status."

"In the telecom stairwell," you murmur. "Top floor. Package is conscious. Barely. I took a graze, it's nothing. Crossing party is on the gantry — one across, one covering, more coming. You have maybe two minutes before they're in this building by another route."

A pause. Then: "Understood. Start down. Slow. Don't reach the lobby before six minutes even. If you're early, hold on a landing. The street needs to be what I'm making it."

"Copy."

The line stays open this time. He's riding it with you.

You crouch in front of Mira. Her eyes drift, find you, focus with effort.

"Twenty floors down," you tell her. "Last set. I carry you."

"…chip," she whispers.

"Against my ribs. Still there."

"If I—"

"You won't."

She almost smiles again. It's the second time she's almost smiled and you are beginning to hate the expression, because it's the face of someone who has already done the arithmetic you keep refusing to do.

You get your arms under her. You lift. Your left thigh complains; you tell it to be quiet; it obeys, for now.

You start down.

Above you, faint through concrete and distance, you hear the hatch groan open.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "They're coming," I state, more to myself. I shift her weight, trying to find a comfortable position. "Just hold on. We're almost there." I begin the descent, one slow, deliberate step at a time.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

You find the carry that works: her knees hooked over your right forearm, her back supported by your left, her head tucked under your chin so her weight rides close to your centerline instead of pulling you sideways. It's a carry for short distances and you have twenty floors of it. You adjust anyway. You'll adjust again in five floors, and five after that, because that's what the body does — it renegotiates the contract until the contract is over.

The first flight goes cleanly. Clean stairwell, clean treads, your boots finding each step with the particular care of a man who knows that a stumble here costs more than a stumble is allowed to cost. Mira's breath is a small warm pulse against your throat. You count it — in, out, in — and as long as you can count it, it's there.

Floor nineteen. Floor eighteen.

Above, the hatch-groan has resolved into footfalls on the ladder. One set. Then — a pause, voices, brief argument in that clipped tongue — a second set committing. Two men through the hatch. The rest, then, are taking the long route: back across the gantry, or down their own stairwell and across at ground level, hunting for a door into this building. Your handler's eight minutes has to beat those men, not these. These two up top, you can handle. Maybe. If they're slow and you're lucky and the stairwell's acoustics lie to them more than they lie to you.

Floor seventeen. Your left thigh has stopped complaining and started doing something worse — it's gone warm and distant, the way an injury does when the body has decided to spend its attention elsewhere. You file that, too. You have a lot of things filed. The drawer is getting full.

Floor sixteen. You pause on the landing. Not to rest — you don't trust yourself to start again if you rest — but to listen. Above, the two sets of boots have reached the top landing, the one you just left. A voice, low. Another answering. They're not running down after you. They're moving carefully, stairwell-clearing pace, weapons up, reading each flight before committing to it. Professionals.

That's good for you. Professional means slow. Professional means they assume you might turn and fire, and they're going to cost themselves time making sure you can't.

You didn't leave the suppressor behind. You have five rounds. They don't know that. Let them not know that for as long as possible.

Floor fifteen. Floor fourteen. Mira makes a small sound — not pain, something smaller, the sound of a person surfacing briefly from somewhere deep and then going back under. Her fingers twitch against your collar.

"Still here," you murmur. "Still holding."

"…mm."

Floor thirteen. Your earpiece clicks.

"Ghost. Talk to me."

"Thirteen," you breathe. "Two following. Slow. Professional pace. Package is unconscious or close."

"Copy thirteen. Don't speed up. I know you want to. Don't."

"Copy."

Floor twelve. You realize you've started sweating — not the cold sweat of the stairwell climb, but the hot sweat of a body that's been running on borrowed chemistry for too long and is starting to charge interest. Your hands are steady. Your legs are steady. Your *head* is the thing that's going to go first, and you know it, and you're watching for it.

Floor eleven. You shift Mira's weight. She's heavier now than she was three floors ago — not really, but effectively, the arithmetic of fatigue. You hook her knees higher on your forearm. Her face lolls against your jaw. Her skin is cool. You don't love how cool.

Floor ten. Halfway.

You pause again, briefly, on the landing, and you listen. The men above are — you estimate — four floors behind you. They've closed slightly. They've decided, correctly, that the target isn't setting ambushes. That's fine. Let them keep deciding that. Let them keep closing at exactly the rate you can tolerate.

Your earpiece: "Five minutes forty to car. You're ahead of pace. Slow it."

You slow it.

Floor nine. Floor eight. You make each step last a full beat now, deliberate, metronomic. Mira's breathing is shallower but still there. You count it against the rhythm of your steps: one step, one breath, one step, one breath. When they don't match up you adjust your pace, not hers. She's not adjusting anything anymore.

Floor seven. A door opens somewhere below you — not in the stairwell, in the building beyond it. A distant pneumatic *hiss* and then the faint drift of a different kind of air: cooler, night-scented, outside-adjacent. Someone has opened an exterior door somewhere in the telecom building. You go still on the landing.

"Handler," you breathe. "Exterior door, lower floors. Not mine?"

A pause. "Not yours. Night staff. Telecom runs a skeleton crew, one of them just stepped out to smoke on the west side. My asset in the lobby sees him. He's nobody. Keep moving."

You keep moving.

Floor six. Floor five. Your left thigh is now actively bleeding again — you can feel the fresh warmth down to the knee — the graze having been reopened by the sustained flexion of the descent. You don't look. You file it in the drawer with everything else.

Floor four. Your earpiece: "Four minutes. Hold on three."

"Copy."

Floor three. You reach the landing, and you stop.

The landing is wider than the others — an inter-floor service level, with a narrow alcove to one side where a fire extinguisher and a coiled hose hang on hooks. You step into the alcove. You turn so your back is to the wall and Mira's body is shielded by yours and the angle of the alcove, and the stairwell above you is covered by your sightline over her shoulder.

You draw the pistol with your right hand — awkward, because your right arm is still supporting her knees, but you manage. You rest the muzzle on the crook of your left elbow where it crosses her back. The sight picture finds the last flight of stairs above you and holds there.

Five rounds. Two men. Stairwell acoustics. If they come into view while you're holding here, you have maybe a second and a half of clean shot before they process what they're looking at and go to cover. You don't love those odds but you don't hate them either.

Mira's breath, against your throat. Still there. Thin.

You wait.

Above, the boots have paused. They've reached a landing — eighth, seventh, you can't tell exactly — and they're doing what you did: listening. Deciding whether the silence below them is empty stairwell or baited one.

Your earpiece, very quietly: "Three minutes fifty. Hold."

You hold.

The stairwell holds its breath with you. Somewhere far below, a door hums in its frame. Somewhere far above, a man decides something, and a boot finds the next tread, and the descent resumes — careful, unhurried, closer.

You don't move. You don't breathe harder than you need to. The pistol's front sight sits on the edge of the flight above, exactly where a knee will appear before a torso does, exactly where you want it.

Three minutes forty. Three minutes thirty.

The first boot enters your sight picture.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *Damn it.* I squeeze the trigger.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

The pistol coughs.

The round takes him in the shin — you were aiming for the knee, but he was moving and you were holding a woman against your chest with the same arm that braces your pistol, and a shin is what physics gives you. It is enough. The leg goes out from under him like a kicked chair and he drops, hard, onto the flight. His rifle clatters against the rail. He makes a sound that is not a scream — professionals don't scream, not at first — but is the compressed grunt of a man converting pain into tactics in real time.

He's down but he's not out of the fight. He's rolling toward the inside wall, trying to get below your sightline, and his partner — *his partner* — is the problem you actually have.

The partner reacts fast. Faster than you hoped. There's a shout, sharp and short, and then the second man is no longer on the stairs — he's dropped back up the flight, out of sight, which means he's now on the landing above and he knows exactly where you are, because your muzzle flash just told him, and the suppressor kept the sound vague but the flash did not.

You have maybe two seconds before he comes back with a different angle.

You fire again — not at anything, at the wall above the downed man, a single round that smacks concrete and throws chips. Suppressing fire, the cheapest kind, the kind that costs you a round you can't afford and buys you a heartbeat of hesitation from a man who was about to lean into your sightline. Three rounds left.

You move.

You step out of the alcove, pivot, and go *down* — not walking now, not careful, a controlled half-fall down the flight, your left hand abandoning the pistol grip to catch the rail and brake Mira's weight against the momentum. Your bad thigh screams and this time you hear it. Her head bounces against your collarbone. Three steps, four, the landing of floor two, around the turn, out of the line of sight from above.

Behind and above, the second man is already firing. Unsuppressed. The cracks are enormous in the stairwell, a physical pressure against your ears, and rounds chew the wall where you were standing a second ago. Concrete dust blooms. The downed man on the flight is shouting — directions, corrections, the particular urgency of a wounded professional telling his partner where the target went.

Floor two. You don't stop. You don't listen to your earpiece, which is saying something — your handler's voice, tight, asking — because listening costs attention and you have none to spare.

Floor one. The stairwell opens onto a short corridor and, at the end of it, a glass door with the bored white light of a lobby beyond. You see, through the glass, the shape of a desk, the shape of a man behind it — your handler's asset, presumably — looking up sharply at the sound of gunfire three floors above his head.

You hit the corridor at something that is not quite a run and not quite a walk, Mira's weight throwing your gait off on every other step. Your left leg is wet to the boot now. You don't look.

Your earpiece, finally breaking through: "—sitrep, Ghost, *sitrep*—"

"One down, one active, I'm in the lobby corridor, *open the street*."

A beat. Then, flat and fast: "Street opens in ninety seconds. Not sooner. You hold in the lobby. My asset will cover the stairwell door behind you. *Ninety seconds.*"

Ninety seconds is a long time with an active shooter three floors up and descending.

You hit the glass door with your shoulder. It swings. The lobby is small — telecom buildings don't do grand — a polished stone floor, a reception desk, two uncomfortable chairs, a bank of elevators on the left wall, and the main entrance on the right: double glass doors onto Kuznetsova Street, currently dark, currently locked, currently *not yet* the exit you need.

The man at the desk is already on his feet. Mid-fifties, unremarkable, the kind of face that disappears in a crowd of three. He has a pistol in his hand, held low and professional, and he is not pointing it at you. He is pointing it at the stairwell door you just came through.

"Down," he says, in accented English. "Behind the desk. I have the door."

You carry Mira around the desk and lower her to the floor in the footwell, where the wood of the desk's front is between her body and the stairwell. You prop her against the inside panel. Her head tips. Her eyes are closed now. You press two fingers to her throat.

Pulse. Still. Thready. Still.

You rise. You reload nothing — you have three rounds and no spare magazine, the mission profile hadn't called for one — and you take position at the corner of the desk, pistol up, sighting on the stairwell door you came through. The asset is on the opposite corner, same posture, covering the same door from a different angle. Crossfire. Good.

Your earpiece: "Eighty seconds."

Through the stairwell door, muffled by the glass and the corridor, you hear boots. Descending. Not careful anymore. The second man has decided that careful is costing him the target and has shifted modes. He's coming fast.

You settle your front sight on the center of the stairwell door. You breathe. Your hands, against all reasonable expectation, are steady.

Seventy seconds.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I nod to the desk clerk. "Good. Seventy seconds." I focus on the door, holding my breath.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
Narrator:

The asset nods back. A small, economical motion, the kind a man makes when he has done this before and the doing of it does not require conversation. His pistol is a CZ, you note distantly — the observation arrives unbidden, the way your brain handles stress by cataloguing trivia — and he holds it the way someone taught him, thumb-forward, no drift. He's real. Whoever your handler is paying, he's paying for real.

Sixty-five seconds.

The boots in the stairwell grow. You track them by the rhythm of impact on concrete — he's taking the stairs three at a time now, the last flight, no longer clearing corners because he knows the corner is the lobby and the lobby is where you are. You feel the shape of his approach in the soles of your feet.

Sixty seconds.

You adjust your grip. The front sight sits center-mass on the door, at the height a man's chest will be when he comes through it. You have three rounds. The asset has — you glance, one flick — a full magazine, probably fifteen, plus whatever's in the chamber. Between the two of you, the door is not a door he survives coming through. You both know it. He knows you know it. That's the geometry.

The question is whether he comes through it anyway.

Fifty-five seconds.

The boots hit the bottom landing. Stop. You hear — very faintly, through two doors and a corridor — the scrape of a shoulder against a wall as he takes cover beside the stairwell door on his side. He is doing now what you did three floors ago: listening. Deciding.

If he's smart, he doesn't come through. If he's smart, he holds that door and waits for his friends — the ones who took the long route, the ones who are, right now, somewhere between this building's unopened exterior doors and the street outside. If he holds, he pins you in the lobby. If he holds, *ninety seconds* becomes *until his friends arrive*, and *until his friends arrive* is a number your handler did not promise.

He's smart.

Fifty seconds.

Your earpiece, very low: "Ghost. My asset sees a van on the east side. Two out, moving to the service entrance. You have less time than I sold you. I'm compressing. Sixty seconds to car. Sixty."

Sixty. Not ninety. The math just changed on you.

Behind the desk, Mira's head has slipped sideways against the wood. You don't look. You can't look. Looking is a decision and you're out of decisions that aren't the door.

Forty-five seconds.

The asset's eyes meet yours across the desk. He's heard the same transmission — he's on the same earpiece channel, of course he is — and his face does a small thing that is almost, but not quite, a smile. It is the expression of a man who has just been told his job got shorter and harder in the same sentence, and who has decided, privately, that this is acceptable.

He tips his head toward the main entrance. The double glass doors. Kuznetsova Street beyond, dark and wet and waiting.

"When the car comes," he murmurs, not taking his eyes off the stairwell door, "you go. I stay."

You don't answer. There is no answer to that sentence that is not either a lie or an insult, so you give him neither.

Forty seconds.

Somewhere in the building — east side, service entrance — you hear, muffled through the bones of the structure, a door give way. Not opening. Giving. The particular sound of a lock losing an argument with a tool designed to win it.

The two men from the van are in.

Thirty-five seconds.

The asset's posture shifts fractionally. He's now dividing his attention — left eye on the stairwell door, right eye toward the corridor that leads from the lobby into the rest of the ground floor, because that's where the new arrivals will come from. You do the opposite: you commit fully to the stairwell door, because if the man behind it decides to stop being smart in the next thirty seconds, you are the one who has to answer him.

Mira's breath. You can't hear it over the blood in your ears, but the desk wood between you vibrates faintly when she exhales. Still there. Still there. Still there.

Thirty seconds.

Your earpiece: "Car is turning onto Kuznetsova. Black sedan, no plates visible, driver is mine. When I say *move*, you move. Not before."

"Copy."

Twenty-five seconds.

From the corridor to your right, footsteps. Two sets. Not running — *clearing*, the measured advance of men who know the building and know the lobby is the terminus. The asset's pistol tracks that way without his eyes following, a small professional miracle of peripheral aim.

From the stairwell door, still — stillness. The smart man is still being smart. He's waiting for his friends to arrive before he opens the door, because a door opened in coordination with a flank is a door that works, and a door opened alone is a door that gets you shot by two people behind a desk.

You have maybe eight seconds before both those doors become the same problem at the same moment.

Twenty seconds.

"Handler," you breathe. "They're coordinating. Stairwell and corridor. Eight seconds, maybe."

A pause. Then, fast: "Asset — corridor. Ghost — stairwell. When they move, you both fire through the doors. Don't wait for targets. The doors are the targets. *Suppress, don't aim.* Car arrives in fifteen. Side door of the sedan will be open. You break for it on my mark, package in your arms, do not stop, do not return fire from the street — my driver has the street. Acknowledge."

"Acknowledged," you breathe.

The asset, softer: "Da."

Fifteen seconds.

You shift your aim from center-of-door to lower-left-of-door — the hinge side, the side a man's cover-thin shoulder would press against as he leans through. You don't need to hit him. You need the door itself to become a thing he does not want to be near.

The footsteps in the corridor stop. Exactly where you'd stop, if you were them. Just outside the lobby's sightline, staging.

Ten seconds.

Somewhere out on Kuznetsova Street, very faintly through the glass of the main doors, you hear a car engine. Coming closer. Slowing.

Nine.

The stairwell door handle turns. A quarter of an inch. Testing.

Eight.

Your earpiece: "*Move on three.*"

Seven.

"Three—"

Six.

"—two—"

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "—one." I fire through the door. I don't wait to see if it hits, I'm already scooping Mira up. "Let's go!"

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: The Narrator maintains exceptional consistency throughout - the tense, professional spy thriller tone never wavers, the detailed tactical descriptions remain precise, and the character voices (especially the handler's fear, Mira's deteriorating condition) stay true from start to finish.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality remains high throughout. Early turns show excellent scene-setting and tension, middle turns maintain tactical precision and emotional weight, final turns deliver climactic intensity without becoming overwrought. No significant verbosity or repetition creep.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Excellent progression from initial escape through stairwell ascent, roof crossing, building descent, to final lobby standoff. Each turn escalates stakes naturally - Mira's condition worsens, pursuers close in, time pressure mounts. Clear three-act structure with rising tension.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: Narrator adapts well to user choices - when user shoots lights, consequences follow logically; when user checks stairwell, detailed tactical assessment provided; when user makes quick decisions under pressure, pacing accelerates appropriately. Handles user's terse, action-focused style well.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Generally respects user agency well. Occasionally describes user's internal thoughts/feelings ('you realize,' 'you file that away') but doesn't control major actions. User drives key decisions throughout.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Time flows consistently - the 11-minute countdown to extraction is tracked well, Mira's blood loss progresses realistically, physical exhaustion accumulates naturally. Handler's timeline compression from 90 to 60 seconds creates appropriate urgency.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Prose is precise and functional, matching the tactical thriller genre. Descriptions are detailed but serve tension and atmosphere rather than being ornamental. Occasional poetic touches ('the stairwell breathes') enhance rather than distract.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Avoids repetitive phrasing well. Tactical descriptions vary in structure, emotional beats are expressed differently each time, and the constant tension is maintained through varied language rather than repeated phrases.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing throughout - Mira's deteriorating condition shown through physical details (grey skin, weak pulse, slipping consciousness), tension shown through environmental details (alarm lights, footsteps, radio static), fear shown through handler's voice changes.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Strong subtext throughout - the handler's fear implies larger stakes, Mira's 'almost smiles' suggest acceptance of likely death, the chip's significance conveyed through reactions rather than exposition. Professional relationships conveyed through minimal dialogue.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Excellent pacing that matches the urgency of the situation. Quick tactical moments are terse, climbing sequences have appropriate rhythm, final countdown builds tension effectively. Varies sentence length and paragraph structure to control tempo.

scores.overall_notes: This is a strong spy thriller roleplay that maintains tension, consistency, and quality throughout. The Narrator excels at tactical detail, character consistency, and building suspense through environmental storytelling. The session has clear momentum from escape to extraction, with each turn raising stakes naturally. Minor issues with occasional internal narration, but overall respects user agency well. The writing style perfectly matches the genre and maintains professional competence throughout.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You hear the alarm before you see the lights. Red, pulsing, flooding the corridor in waves. The defector — Mira, she tells you her name is Mira — leans heavier on your shoulder. Her left leg isn't working right. You don't look at it.

The stairwell door ahead is still clear. For now. You count floors in your head: four to the garage, three to the service tunnel, two to certain capture. The radio in your ear gives you static. Your handler has gone silent.

"How many?" Mira asks. Her voice is steady. Steadier than yours would be.

You don't answer. You move.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I check the stairwell. Listen for footsteps below before committing.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You ease the stairwell door open a finger's width.

Cold air hits your face. The concrete shaft amplifies every sound into a question. For three seconds, nothing — just the blood in your ears, Mira's shallow breathing behind you. Then it comes: rhythmic, metallic. Footfalls. A full squad by the pattern, four floors down but climbing. Their pace is unhurried. They don't know you're here. Not yet.

Above you, silence. The garage level waits at the top of the shaft, forty-eight steps away. The alternative is the service tunnel three floors down, the same direction as those boots.

Mira shifts her weight. Her fingers dig into your jacket. She doesn't ask again.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *I pull Mira closer, pressing her against the wall next to the door.* "Quiet," *I whisper, my eyes scanning the silent upper floors.* "We're going up."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You push through the door and the shaft swallows you both.

The stairwell smells of damp concrete and old diesel. Emergency lights run red seams along the walls. Your boots find the first step and Mira matches you — slow, then slower, her bad leg dragging a beat behind. You count silently. Twelve steps to the landing. The squad below hasn't changed pace. Their voices drift up now, casual, a language you don't speak.

Third floor landing. You pause. Mira's forehead presses against your shoulder blade. Hot through the fabric. Fever or fear — you don't know which yet.

Four floors to garage. Three to the tunnel. Two to certain capture. You're halfway to safe ground when a door slams somewhere beneath you, and the footfalls stop.

Then they change direction.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I hear them coming from below. I reverse direction — up, not down. I pull Mira with me.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
The decision is made before you finish thinking it.

You haul Mira upward. The concrete steps blur under the emergency lights. Your thighs burn by the fourth-floor landing. Mira gasps with every other step but she doesn't slow you down — she's matching your rhythm now, three-beat, two-beat, her good leg doing the work of both. The radio in your ear crackles once, a syllable cut in half, then dies again.

Fifth floor. Almost there.

Below you, the squad has stopped their casual chatter. Their boots are faster now. Echoes bounce wrong in the shaft — it's hard to tell how close. Thirty seconds behind, maybe less.

The garage door looms at the top of the final flight. Painted steel. No handle on this side, just a magnetic lock with a keypad, black plastic, small red light blinking. You don't have the code.

Behind the door, you hear an engine start.

Mira's grip on your jacket tightens. The squad below hits the third-floor landing.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *I ignore the keypad, knowing it's useless.* "Stay here," *I grunt, pulling a small pry bar from my kit.* "I'll get us through."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You jam the pry bar between the door and the frame, just above the magnetic lock. The steel groans. Paint flakes onto your knuckles.

The red light on the keypad keeps blinking.

Below, a voice barks something sharp. Boots accelerate on the stairs. Third floor landing, maybe fourth. The echoes lie to you. You lean your weight into the bar, both hands, and the gap widens by a millimeter — then two — then the lock's casing squeals, a sound like a wounded animal. Your shoulders shake. Mira presses herself flat against the wall, her eyes on the stairwell, her chest hardly moving.

The pry bar slips. You catch it before it clatters. Reset, regrip, pull again.

The magnet releases with a sudden, violent pop. The door swings outward into humid garage air, and the engine sound you heard is a black SUV idling fifteen feet away, exhaust curling around its tires, driver's seat empty.

Headlights snap on from the far ramp. A second vehicle. Coming your way.

Mira is already moving, stumbling through the gap before you can reach for her. You follow, boots hitting oil-stained concrete, and the stairwell behind you erupts with the squad's final sprint.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I set Mira down in the server room and barricade the door. I need to think.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
The server room swallows you in cold, sterile air. Rows of black racks hum a low, constant chord. Blue status lights wink through the grated floor like grounded stars. You lower Mira against a cabinet, and she slides down its face without protest, her bad leg stretched awkwardly, her eyes half-closed.

You move fast. A heavy rolling cart loaded with dead hard drives — you wedge it against the door, then tip a steel shelving unit onto its side. The crash is swallowed by the server hum. Sparks spit from a severed power strip, then die.

Through the door, muffled: shouts in the garage. Tires squeal on concrete. A door slams. The squad from the stairwell has spilled out into the open, and now they're hunting blind. Flashlight beams sweep across the narrow slit under the door, then move on.

No boot kicks the barricade. Not yet.

Mira's head lolls toward you. Sweat plasters her hair to her temples. She's saying something, but the server hum eats her voice. You crouch close. Her lips barely move.

"...left them a trail."

She's right. You know she's right. The stairwell door behind you hangs open. The blood from her leg has dripped across twelve flights of concrete. It's only a matter of time before someone puts a boot in that red smear and follows it here. The radio in your ear stays silent. Your handler is either compromised or dead.

You have minutes, maybe less. The server racks gleam around you, processing data you don't have the key to read, humming their endless, indifferent song.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I check Mira's leg. "What trail?" I ask, then scan the room for anything useful. This isn't good.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You peel Mira's torn pant leg back and the sight hits you before the smell does. A gash runs across her calf, deep and clean-edged — glass, maybe, from a window you didn't see her break. The blood is dark in the blue server light, spreading in a slow, steady pulse. Not arterial. That's the only good news.

"They gave me a tracer," Mira whispers. Her head sags forward, chin to chest. "Injection. My thigh. Before your people grabbed me."

You find it with your fingers: a hard lump under the skin, small as a grain of rice. Still transmitting. Still leading every hostile in the building straight to this door.

The server room offers you no exits. No vents big enough for a body. No windows. Just miles of cabling, cold air, and the heavy steel door groaning against your barricade.

But your eyes land on something else. In the corner, rack seven. A maintenance panel hangs open. Inside: a portable diagnostics rig, military-grade, still powered on. And coiled beneath it, a shielded cable clean enough to carry a localized EMP burst if you can strip it right. Short-range. Just enough to fry the tracer and every radio within twenty feet.

Including yours.

The flashlight beams sweep past the door again. Closer now. A fist hammers the steel twice, testing.

"Occupied?" a voice calls in accented English. Then silence. Then the handle rattles.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *I ignore the thumping on the door for a second, my mind racing.* "A tracer? Damn it." I look at the diagnostics rig. "This is it. We're doing it."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You lunge for the maintenance panel.

The diagnostics rig is heavier than it looks — a suitcase-sized block of military plastic and copper cooling fins. You drag it free. Cables snarl and snap. A blue status screen blinks at you, asking for a password you don't have time to type. You ignore it, flip the rig onto its back, and expose the power regulation board. The shielded cable underneath is thick as your thumb, wrapped in foil and braided mesh. Perfect.

Behind you, the door handle rattles again. Harder. Someone outside shouts, and two more voices answer.

Mira drags herself upright against the cabinet. Her face is the color of old paper. "They're going to breach."

You strip the cable with your knife. Copper gleams in the blue light. The server hum fills your skull. Your hands move without permission from your brain — muscle memory from a training course you hoped you'd never need. Twist the leads. Bridge the capacitor. Loop the shielding back on itself. The rig's power supply whines as you reroute its output into a single, unstable loop. Twenty feet of death for anything with a circuit. Including the tracer. Including your radio. Including every server rack in this room.

The barricade shudders. A crowbar slides through the gap between door and frame. Metal screams against metal.

"Now," Mira breathes.

You thumb the makeshift trigger.

The EMP is silent. That's the unsettling part. No flash, no bang, no visible evidence that anything has changed — except every blue light in the room dies at once. The server hum drops into absolute quiet. The diagnostics rig goes dark in your hands. The radio in your ear spits a burst of static and then nothing, not even a carrier wave. Just dead air. You are alone now. Truly alone.

Mira gasps. Her hand flies to her thigh. The hard lump under her skin is still there, but inert — a grain of rice gone cold.

Outside the door, the shouting changes pitch. Radios crackle into confusion. Flashlight beams swing wild, then steady. The crowbar withdraws with a screech of tortured steel.

Silence. The barricade holds. The squad outside is blind, deaf, and for the first time since you entered this building, you have a head start they don't know they've given you.

But the server room is dead, and so is every exit route that required a keypad or a signal. You are three floors above the service tunnel and five above the street, in a darkening box of dead machines, with a woman who can't walk and a door that won't hold forever.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "Good," I whisper, looking at Mira. "That's one problem solved." I push the heavy diagnostics rig aside, then start scanning the now-dark server room. "Okay, now we need to find another way out."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You let your eyes adjust.

The darkness isn't total. Emergency strips along the floor still hold a dim chemical glow — green, sickly, just enough to trace outlines. The server racks loom like headstones. The dead diagnostics rig smokes faintly at your feet, its capacitors spent.

Mira pushes hair from her face with a shaking hand. "The floor," she says. "I felt it when we came in. A seam."

You drop to a crouch. Your palms skate across cold grating until they find it: a recessed maintenance hatch, flush with the floor panels, sealed with four hex bolts. No power required. No signal to spoof. Just steel and muscle.

Outside the door, the squad is regrouping. You hear their boots retreating — but not far. They'll post a guard. They'll wait for reinforcements with working radios. Your window is measured in minutes.

You fish a multitool from your kit and set to work on the first bolt. The threading is old, gummy with dust, but it turns. So does the second. The third resists. The fourth won't budge.

Mira watches the door. Her breathing is too fast. "When they come back—"

"They won't wait long," you finish.

Bolt four gives with a shriek of oxidized metal. You hook your fingers into the recessed ring and pull. The hatch rises with a gasp of stale air — and something else. Oil. Solvent. The chemical sweetness of a laundry line.

This isn't a maintenance shaft. It's a laundry chute. Wide enough for a body. Straight down.

In the dark below, you hear water dripping.

The squad's voices rise outside. A finality in their tone. They've made a decision. Boots approach the door again — heavier this time. Deliberate. A breaching ram, by the sound of the first impact against the barricade.

Mira looks at you. The green glow paints hollows under her eyes. "How far down?"

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "Doesn't matter." *I grab Mira's good arm.* "You first."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You half-lift, half-push Mira into the chute.

She doesn't hesitate. That's the thing about her — for a woman running on one leg and borrowed time, she commits. Her shoulders clear the opening, then her hips. She braces her good foot against the rim, looks up at you with eyes that have already accepted whatever's at the bottom, and lets go.

The chute takes her. Fabric rustles against metal. Her descent fades fast — three seconds, four — and ends with a wet crash and a grunt that echoes up through the dark.

"I'm clear," she calls. Her voice is small, distant, distorted by the shaft. "Water. Maybe knee deep. It's a room."

Behind you, the barricade splinters.

The breaching ram hits the door a second time and the rolling cart buckles inward. Steel shelving tilts. A gap opens at the top of the frame, wide enough for a hand to reach through, and a black-gloved fist follows — gripping, searching. You don't wait to see what it finds.

You swing your legs into the chute. Cold air rushes past you. The walls are smooth sheet metal, riveted at the seams, slick with decades of soap residue and fabric lint. You push off before the fist behind you finds a purchase.

The fall is faster than you expect.

Your shoulders bang the sides twice. The chute angles, then drops vertical. You can't slow yourself. The air smells like bleach and mildew and something rotting. Your boots catch nothing. Then the chute spits you out into open space and you land hard, water exploding around you, your ankle twisting on something submerged and solid.

The room is a basement laundry. Old industrial machines sit in flooded rows, their chrome faces green with oxidation. The water is black in the dark, warm as bathwater, and it stinks of stagnant detergent and rust. A single emergency light flickers above a bolted door on the far wall. The exit.

Mira stands a few feet away, leaning on a tilted washing machine, her bad leg lifted out of the water. She points at the door.

"Maintenance corridor. Leads to the service tunnel." She knows the building better than she told you. You file that away.

Above, distant and muffled, the server room door finally gives. Boots pound across the grating. Flashlight beams stab down the chute, fractionally too late. You're already moving, already hauling Mira through the black water, already seeing the seam of the service tunnel door glowing faintly ahead — yellow, cheap fluorescent, the most beautiful light you've ever seen.

The radio in your ear is still dead. No handler. No extraction plan. Just you, the defector, and three floors of flooded basement between this moment and the street.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *I pull Mira closer, my arm around her waist, and half-drag her towards the door.* "Let's go. Don't stop now."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You drag Mira through the flood.

The water resists every step. Your boots churn up silt and old chemical sludge. Something soft bumps your ankle and you don't think about what it might be. Mira's arm locks around your neck. Her bad leg cuts a wake behind her. The emergency light buzzes overhead, sputtering, threatening to quit entirely.

The door is closer now. Six feet. Four. You can read the stenciled letters: MAINTENANCE CORRIDOR B — AUTHORIZED ACCESS ONLY. The yellow light bleeds through a wire-reinforced window set in the door's upper half. Beyond it, a hallway. Dry floor. A straight shot to the service tunnel if Mira's intel holds.

A crash echoes down the laundry chute behind you. Voices bounce after it. Someone up there has found the opening. You don't look back.

You hit the door with your shoulder. It swings outward, rusted hinges screaming, and you spill into the corridor with Mira half-collapsed in your grip. The floor here is cracked linoleum, mercifully dry. Fluorescent tubes hum overhead. The hallway stretches forty feet in either direction — left toward a dead-end breaker room, right toward a T-junction lit by an exit sign's soft red halo.

Mira coughs. She pulls herself upright against the wall, leaving a wet handprint on the paint. "Right," she manages. "Then two lefts. The tunnel access is behind a false panel in the electrical closet."

Above you, through the laundry room door you left hanging open, a flashlight beam sweeps across the flooded floor. They're coming down the chute. You have maybe ninety seconds before the first boots hit the water.

You pull Mira to her feet. She doesn't complain.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "Got it." *I keep a hand on Mira's arm, urging her forward.* "Let's move."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You move.

The corridor stretches ahead, linoleum peeling in long strips that curl under your boots. Mira keeps pace despite the limp — she's found a rhythm now, a lurching three-step that favors her good leg and uses the wall as a crutch. Her breath comes in tight, controlled hisses. You don't tell her to hurry. She knows.

First left. The hallway narrows. Pipes run along the ceiling here, sweating condensation that drips onto your shoulders. The fluorescent light gives way to bare bulbs strung at intervals, and shadows pool in the gaps between them. Your own shadow stretches ahead, then collapses, then stretches again — a dark twin you can't shake.

Second left. The electrical closet stands at the end of a short dead-end spur, its door painted utility gray, a faded lightning bolt stenciled at eye level. No lock. You twist the handle and pull.

The room is barely six feet square. Breaker panels cover two walls, their switches all thrown to OFF — dead circuits you killed with your pulse upstairs. A mop bucket sits in the corner, filled with black water and a single cigarette butt floating on the surface. The air smells of ozone and old tobacco.

"Back wall," Mira gasps. "Left of the main panel. The seam runs vertical."

You find it with your fingers: a hairline crack in the drywall, too straight to be accidental. You press, and a section the size of a door swings inward on hidden hinges. Beyond it — darkness, and the cool, moving air of a deeper space. The service tunnel.

Behind you, in the corridor you just left, a door slams open. Boots on linoleum. Voices, sharp and close. They've cleared the laundry room. They're in the hallway.

You push Mira through the false panel. She stumbles, catches herself on a rusted ladder rung, and starts down without being told. You follow, pulling the panel shut behind you — but it doesn't seat flush. A sliver of light remains, a vertical line thin as a knife blade. Anyone who checks the closet will see it.

The tunnel swallows you both. The ladder descends fifteen feet to a concrete floor, and Mira is already at the bottom when your boots hit the ground. The space is pre-war infrastructure — brick archways, iron support beams, a channel of slow-moving wastewater down the center. Rats scatter in the beam of your pocket flashlight. The air is cold here, cold enough to raise goosebumps on your wet skin.

A hundred yards ahead, the tunnel terminates at a maintenance grate. Street level. An exit. You can smell the city above: exhaust, rain, night air.

Mira sinks against the brick wall. Her face in the flashlight beam is gray, her lips bloodless. The gash on her calf has started bleeding again, thin rivulets cutting through the grime. But her eyes are clear. She points toward the grate.

You pull her up. She doesn't resist. You move into the dark together, the tunnel narrowing around you, every step carrying you closer to the surface. Above, through layers of concrete and earth, you can't hear the squad anymore. You don't know if that means you've lost them, or if they're simply waiting where you're headed.

The grate looms ahead. Rusted iron, a simple padlock on the inside. You pull the multitool from your kit. Your hands are steady now. The adrenaline has burned itself into something colder, something you can use.

Mira watches the tunnel behind you. "They'll have the street covered."

"I know."

The padlock gives. You push the grate open, and cold rain hits your face. You're in an alley, narrow and dark, reeking of garbage and wet asphalt. A single streetlamp flickers at the mouth of the alley, painting yellow lines across the pavement. Beyond it, a city street. Traffic. People. Cover.

But also — black sedans. Two of them, idling curbside, windows tinted, exhaust curling in the cold air. Waiting.

Mira sees them. She doesn't say anything.

You're out of the building. Out of the basement. But you're not clear. Not yet.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *I pull Mira closer into the shadows of the alley, away from the streetlamp.* "No time to waste," *I mutter, already scanning the alley for another way out, or something to use as a distraction.* "Stay low."

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: The Narrator maintains a consistently tense, cinematic voice throughout all 12 turns. The writing style remains noir-influenced with precise, atmospheric descriptions. Character voice never wavers or becomes generic.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality remains strong throughout. Early turns (1-5) establish excellent atmosphere and pacing. Later turns (18-23) maintain the same level of detail and tension without becoming verbose or repetitive. No significant quality drop detected.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Excellent progression from initial escape through multiple escalating obstacles: stairwell pursuit, garage confrontation, server room barricade, EMP solution, laundry chute escape, tunnel navigation, to final alley emergence. Each turn raises stakes and advances the story meaningfully.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: AI adapts well to user choices: when user goes up instead of down (turn 5), when user uses pry bar (turn 8), when user triggers EMP (turn 14). Responds naturally to user's tactical decisions while maintaining story logic.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Excellent respect for user agency throughout. Never writes the Operative's actions or decisions. Always responds to what the user chooses to do rather than railroading. Zero violations detected.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Time flows consistently and logically. The chase sequence maintains realistic pacing - squad movements, climbing stairs, EMP effects, descent through chute all feel appropriately timed. No contradictions found.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Writing is atmospheric but controlled. Descriptions serve the tension and mood without becoming overwrought. Phrases like 'blue status lights wink through the grated floor like grounded stars' are evocative but not excessive.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Varied sentence structure and descriptions throughout. Each location (stairwell, garage, server room, laundry, tunnel) has distinct atmospheric details. No noticeable repetitive phrases or structures.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing throughout: 'Her fingers dig into your jacket' shows desperation, 'The squad below hasn't changed pace' shows they're unaware, 'Mira's forehead presses against your shoulder blade. Hot through the fabric' shows her condition without stating it.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Strong subtext in character interactions. Mira's knowledge of the building layout hints at more than she's revealed. The dead radio suggests handler compromise. Tension between characters communicated through action and brief dialogue.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Excellent pacing that matches the urgency of the situation. Short, punchy sentences during action sequences. Longer descriptions during brief respites. Each turn maintains momentum while providing necessary detail.

scores.overall_notes: This is a strong thriller/espionage roleplay with consistent quality throughout. The Narrator maintains excellent atmosphere, respects user agency completely, and builds genuine tension through each escalating obstacle. The writing style is cinematic and engaging without becoming purple. Minor areas for improvement include occasionally dense description blocks, but overall this represents high-quality collaborative storytelling.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You hear the alarm before you see the lights. Red, pulsing, flooding the corridor in waves. The defector — Mira, she tells you her name is Mira — leans heavier on your shoulder. Her left leg isn't working right. You don't look at it.

The stairwell door ahead is still clear. For now. You count floors in your head: four to the garage, three to the service tunnel, two to certain capture. The radio in your ear gives you static. Your handler has gone silent.

"How many?" Mira asks. Her voice is steady. Steadier than yours would be.

You don't answer. You move.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I check the stairwell. Listen for footsteps below before committing.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You ease the heavy fire door open just a fraction of an inch. The metal is cold against your palm, and the hinges protest with a faint, metallic whine. 

You tilt your head toward the gap, holding your breath to listen. At first, there is only the hum of the emergency lights and the ragged sound of Mira panting behind you. 

Then, it drifts up the central shaft: the unmistakable, synchronized thud of heavy boots striking concrete. The sharp clatter of tactical gear echoes off the walls. Two floors down, maybe three. They are moving fast, ascending in a tight, disciplined formation. A harsh voice barks a command that reverberates up the stairwell, unintelligible but urgent. 

Mira shifts her weight against the doorframe, her face pale in the flashing crimson light. She watches the tension in your shoulders, reading the situation without needing to hear it herself. 

The boots are getting louder. You have seconds before the strike team reaches the landing below yours.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: *Damn it.* "Can you make it up one flight?" I whisper to Mira, already pulling the door shut as quietly as possible. "We're going *up*."

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
The latch clicks into place, sealing you both back in the flashing red corridor just as the heavy boots hit the landing below. 

Mira doesn't hesitate. She swallows hard, beads of sweat standing out on her pale forehead. "I can do it," she breathes. She adjusts her grip on your shoulder, her fingers digging fiercely into your tactical vest. Her knuckles are stark white in the crimson light. 

You pivot, taking the brunt of her weight. The hallway stretches ahead of you, a strobe-lit gauntlet of frosted glass doors and overturned security desks. If you cannot use this stairwell to go up, you have to find another way. At the far end of the corridor, an elevator bank sits dead, but right beside it is the recessed alcove of a maintenance ladder. It is at least fifty yards away. A marathon for someone with a ruined leg.

Behind you, the heavy steel door of the stairwell shudders in its frame. The muffled squeak of tactical hinges echoes through the metal. A harsh voice barks a command right on the other side of the steel barrier. 

They are checking your floor. You are out of time.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: I hear them coming from below. I reverse direction — up, not down. I pull Mira with me.

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
You yank Mira forward just as the stairwell door bursts open behind you. The heavy steel slams against the concrete wall with a deafening crash. Brilliant white flashlight beams slice through the pulsing red glare of the corridor, sweeping frantically over the overturned security desks.

"Contact!" a harsh voice roars. 

You do not look back. You haul Mira down the corridor, aiming for the maintenance alcove at the far end. Every step is an agonizing effort for her. She bites back a scream as her bad leg drags across the carpet, but she keeps her grip on you, driven by pure adrenaline. 

Suppressed gunfire spits from the stairwell. Bullets tear through the drywall to your left, missing you by inches. Plaster dust erupts into the air, creating a blinding, choking fog. You duck lower, shoving Mira behind the cover of a thick structural pillar just as a second volley shatters the frosted glass of an office door ahead. Shards of glass rain down on your shoulders, glittering in the strobe lights.

The maintenance ladder is still twenty yards away, recessed into the dark alcove. 

Behind you, the strike team fans out into the hallway. Their heavy boots crunch over the broken glass, methodical and relentless. They are using the overturned desks for cover, advancing on your position. 

"I'm slowing you down," Mira gasps. She slumps against the pillar, clutching her thigh. Fresh blood seeps through her fingers, dark and slick in the crimson lighting. Her chest heaves. "Go. Get up the ladder."

The flashlight beams sweep closer, cutting sharply through the settling plaster dust. You have only moments before they flank the pillar.

dialogue.name: Operative
dialogue.content: "No!" I snap. "We go together." *I check my sidearm, glance at the distance to the ladder.* "Can you lean on me and hop? We need to move, now!"

dialogue.name: Narrator
dialogue.content:
